title: Notion-style block editor with per-block OT and cross-session undo
business_context: Product needs Notion-style collaborative document editing where 5-50 users edit the same doc concurrently. Each doc is a tree of blocks (paragraph, heading, list, code). Losing an edit during reconnect is unacceptable — a user typing for 30s offline must merge cleanly on reconnect. Scale: 100k active docs, p99 edit-to-peer-render under 150ms. If this fails: customers lose work, churn spikes, support tickets explode.
constraints: Must preserve causal ordering of edits per block
Reconnect must replay missed ops without duplicates
Undo must work across sessions (user closes laptop, opens phone, hits Cmd+Z)
Must support 50 concurrent editors per doc without quadratic broadcast cost
Server restart cannot lose acknowledged ops
failure_modes_to_handle: Two users edit same block simultaneously
Client reconnects after 10 minutes with 200 buffered ops
Server crashes between op-persist and op-broadcast
Network partition splits editors into two groups
Op arrives out of order due to multi-node Redis adapter latency
architecture_reasoning:
1. Client gesture → local optimistic apply → emit op with clientSeq + parentVersion → server validates against current block version → persists op in Mongo with monotonic serverSeq via findOneAndUpdate $inc → broadcasts to doc room minus origin → peers apply with vector-clock-style reconciliation.
2. Bottleneck is fan-out: 50 editors × 10 ops/sec = 500 ops/sec/doc, ×100k docs = 50M ops/sec aggregate. Single node dies. Must shard docs across nodes via consistent hashing; Redis adapter handles cross-node only for stragglers.
3. MongoDB chosen because (a) ops are a natural append-only collection per doc, (b) change streams give us recovery replay for free, (c) discriminators model heterogeneous block types cleanly, (d) Postgres would force JSONB for block content negating index benefits.
4. Redis adapter for multi-node fan-out; we accept eventual consistency across nodes because per-doc affinity (sticky routing on docId hash) keeps 99% of editors on same node.
5. Recovery: on reconnect, client sends lastServerSeq; server queries ops where serverSeq > lastServerSeq for that doc and replays. Idempotent because (clientId, clientSeq) tuple is unique-indexed.
6. Rejected: Yjs/Automerge CRDT — overkill for block-level granularity and 10x payload cost. Rejected single-server-per-doc routing — creates hotspots for popular docs.

database_design.indexes: Block: (docId, order) compound for ordered fetch; (docId, parentId) for tree walks. Op: (docId, serverSeq) for replay; (docId, clientId, clientSeq) UNIQUE for idempotency dedup; TTL on createdAt for 90-day op log retention.
database_design.queries: Replay: db.ops.find({docId, serverSeq: {$gt: lastSeen}}).sort({serverSeq:1}).limit(1000) — uses compound index, IXSCAN. Apply: findOneAndUpdate on Block with version check ({_id, version: parentVersion} → {$set:{...}, $inc:{version:1}}) returns null on conflict. Op insert uses unique-index conflict as dedup signal (E11000 = already applied).
database_design.transaction_strategy: Per-op transaction: session.startTransaction() → insert op (catch E11000 = idempotent skip) → update block with version check → if block update returns null, abort and tell client to fetch HEAD. ReadConcern: 'majority', WriteConcern: {w:'majority',j:true}. 60s timeout fine — ops are tiny. Multi-doc batch ops use separate transactions per doc to avoid cross-shard 2PC.

socket_io_design.namespaces: /docs namespace — isolates from /presence and /notifications which have different auth scopes and middleware costs.
socket_io_design.rooms: doc:{docId} for edit fan-out. doc:{docId}:presence for cursor positions (separate to allow fine-grained throttling). Joined on 'doc:open' event after permission check.
socket_io_design.events: type ClientOp = { docId: string; blockId: string; clientId: string; clientSeq: number; parentVersion: number; type: 'insert'|'delete'|'update'|'move'; payload: unknown }; type ServerOp = ClientOp & { serverSeq: number; authorId: string; serverTs: number }; Events: 'op:submit' (C→S, ack with {serverSeq}|{conflict, headVersion}), 'op:apply' (S→C broadcast), 'doc:sync' (C→S with lastServerSeq, ack with missed ops array), 'doc:open', 'doc:close'.
socket_io_design.middleware_order: jwt-auth → tenant-scope → rate-limit (200 ops/sec/socket via token bucket in Redis) → payload-size-guard (max 16KB) → doc-permission-check → audit-emit
socket_io_design.adapter_choice: redis — multi-node deployment behind ALB. Use @socket.io/redis-adapter with separate pub/sub Redis (not shared with BullMQ/cache) to avoid head-of-line blocking. shardedPubSub:true on Redis 7+ for horizontal Redis scaling.

race_conditions_addressed: Two clients update same block at same parentVersion: server's findOneAndUpdate version check fails for the second; second client gets conflict + head, resolves locally and resubmits with new parentVersion.
Client retries op after timeout, original op arrived: unique index on (clientId, clientSeq) makes second insert E11000; server returns {duplicate:true} so client doesn't double-apply.
Reconnect arrives before disconnect handler fires on server: socket.io session resume + room rejoin on connect handles this; doc:sync replay catches any missed broadcasts.
Out-of-order op:apply across Redis adapter hops: client tracks max serverSeq seen and ignores any with serverSeq <= lastApplied for that block.
scaling_considerations: 10x (1M docs, 500 editors): Redis adapter pub/sub becomes bottleneck — shard adapter by docId hash across multiple Redis clusters. 100x: per-doc op log grows unbounded; introduce snapshot+truncate (every 1000 ops, write block snapshot, delete ops with serverSeq < snapshot.seq - 200 retained for replay window). Counter collection becomes hot — switch to per-doc HyperLogLog-style sharded counters or per-doc Redis INCR with periodic Mongo persistence.
observability: Metrics: op_latency_ms histogram (p50/p95/p99), op_conflict_rate counter, doc_sockets gauge per node, replay_op_count histogram on doc:sync, redis_adapter_lag gauge. Logs: structured with docId, userId, clientSeq, serverSeq, latencyMs. Traces: OpenTelemetry span per op:submit covering [validate, transaction, broadcast] with attributes. Alerts: conflict_rate > 5% for 5min (likely partition), p99 op_latency > 300ms, doc_sockets imbalance >2σ across nodes (sticky routing broken).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior approach: io.to(room).emit on every keystroke with no persistence, last-writer-wins by timestamp. Breaks at 5 concurrent editors: clock skew causes edits to revert. Breaks on reconnect: no op log = lost typing. Breaks at 2 nodes: no adapter = users on different nodes don't see each other. The killer bug: at 1000 concurrent users, missing rate-limit means one runaway client floods the room with 10k ops/sec, head-of-line blocks every other user on that node.

title: 10k-member channel chat with read receipts, typing throttling, and edit history
business_context: Slack-competitor needs channels up to 10k members. Each channel can be active simultaneously (sales channel during launch). Read receipts must aggregate to avoid 10k×10k = 100M write storm. Typing indicators must not melt the bus. Edits must preserve history for compliance (SOC2). If this fails: enterprise customers won't renew, regulatory issue if message history is mutable.
constraints: Single channel: 10k members, 5k concurrent viewers, 100 msg/sec peak
Read receipts: per-user per-channel last-read, NOT per-message
Typing indicators throttled to 1 broadcast per 3s per user
Edit history immutable, append-only, queryable
Message delivery: at-least-once with client-side dedup
failure_modes_to_handle: Read-receipt write storm when 5k users open channel simultaneously
Typing event flood from one user holding a key
Edit-during-delete race (user edits while another deletes)
LISTEN/NOTIFY connection drops silently
Multi-tab user inflates read counts
architecture_reasoning:
1. Send: client → POST /messages (durable HTTP write) → INSERT with RETURNING → pg_notify('channel:{id}', msg_id) → notifier process LISTENs → fans out via Socket.IO room. HTTP write is intentional: WebSocket is for fan-out, not durable writes (lost on disconnect mid-send).
2. Bottleneck: pg_notify 8KB payload — we only send msg_id, fan-out worker SELECTs the row (hot in shared_buffers). Without this, 100msg/sec × 5k viewers payload-in-notify would saturate Postgres backend.
3. Postgres chosen because: (a) row-level immutable edit history via trigger to messages_history table, (b) advisory locks for edit serialization per message, (c) LISTEN/NOTIFY is purpose-built for this pattern, (d) MongoDB change streams would also work but lose us referential integrity for compliance audit.
4. Redis adapter for multi-node Socket.IO; notifier worker subscribes once per channel and republishes to all nodes via Redis pub/sub independently of Socket.IO adapter (it's data, not socket state).
5. Recovery: client tracks lastMsgId per channel; on connect, GET /channels/{id}/messages?since=lastMsgId.
6. Rejected: per-message read receipts — quadratic. Rejected NOTIFY with full payload — 8KB cliff and bus pressure. Rejected MongoDB — change streams are noisier and no advisory locks.

database_design.indexes: messages (channel_id, id DESC) partial WHERE deleted_at IS NULL — covers history pagination. messages (channel_id, client_msg_id) UNIQUE for idempotency. messages_history (message_id, version) UNIQUE for audit query. channel_reads PK (channel_id, user_id) is the only access pattern.
database_design.queries: History page: SELECT id,author_id,body,version,edited_at FROM messages WHERE channel_id=$1 AND deleted_at IS NULL AND id < $2 ORDER BY id DESC LIMIT 50 — uses partial index, hot. Unread count: SELECT count(*) FROM messages WHERE channel_id=$1 AND id > (SELECT last_read_msg_id FROM channel_reads WHERE channel_id=$1 AND user_id=$2) AND deleted_at IS NULL — cap with LIMIT 100 + '99+' UI. Read update: INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (channel_id,user_id) DO UPDATE SET last_read_msg_id = GREATEST(channel_reads.last_read_msg_id, EXCLUDED.last_read_msg_id), updated_at = now() — monotonic guard prevents multi-tab regression.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Edit: SELECT pg_advisory_xact_lock(hashtext('msg:'|| id)) inside tx → SELECT current row FOR UPDATE → UPDATE with version check (WHERE version = $expected) → trigger snapshots → COMMIT. Read receipt: single UPSERT, no tx needed (atomic). Send: single INSERT with ON CONFLICT (channel_id, client_msg_id) DO NOTHING RETURNING id — returns NULL on duplicate, server re-fetches existing id.

socket_io_design.namespaces: /chat for messages, /presence for typing+online. Splitting keeps typing flood from blocking message handler event loop.
socket_io_design.rooms: chan:{channelId} for message broadcast, chan:{channelId}:typing for typing (smaller — only currently-typing emits). Joined on subscribe event after membership check.
socket_io_design.events: type Msg = { id:string; channelId:string; authorId:string; body:string; version:number; createdAt:string; editedAt?:string }; type TypingEvt = { channelId:string; userId:string; expiresAt:number }; Events: 'msg:new' (S→C), 'msg:edited' (S→C), 'msg:deleted' (S→C), 'typing:start' (C→S), 'typing:active' (S→C broadcast), 'read:update' (C→S, no ack-required), 'subscribe' (C→S, ack with cursor).
socket_io_design.middleware_order: jwt-auth → tenant-scope → channel-membership-check → rate-limit (typing: 1/3s/user, read: 10/s/user, msg: 5/s/user) → audit
socket_io_design.adapter_choice: redis — multi-node. Notifier worker is separate process: subscribes pg LISTEN, publishes to internal Redis pub/sub channel 'msg:chan:{id}' which Socket.IO server processes consume and rebroadcast via io.to(room).emit. Decouples Postgres backend connection count from Socket.IO node count.

race_conditions_addressed: Duplicate send on retry: client_msg_id UUID + UNIQUE(channel_id, client_msg_id) makes INSERT idempotent; ON CONFLICT returns existing row, client dedups by clientMsgId.
Concurrent edit by two admins: pg_advisory_xact_lock(hashtext('msg:id')) serializes inside tx; version check ensures second admin sees conflict.
Read receipt regression from slow tab: GREATEST(existing, new) in UPSERT ensures last_read_msg_id is monotonic per user.
Typing flood: in-memory token bucket (userId, channelId) drops repeats within 3s without hitting Redis — typing is best-effort so server-local rate-limit is correct.

Multi-server fan-out duplicates: notifier publishes msgId once per Postgres NOTIFY; Redis pub/sub delivers to each Socket.IO node once; node fans to its sockets — no double delivery because subscription is per-node not per-socket.
scaling_considerations: 10x (100k members, 50k concurrent): single Postgres write-leader becomes bottleneck at 1000 msg/sec — partition messages by channel_id hash to multiple Citus shards or use Postgres logical replication to scale reads. LISTEN/NOTIFY single-process bottleneck — run N notifier processes each LISTENing to channel-id mod N. 100x: read-receipt UPSERT contention on hot channels — switch to write-behind via BullMQ batched every 1s per (channel, user) pair.
observability: Metrics: msg_publish_to_deliver_ms (NOTIFY ts → client ack), pg_listen_lag_ms, typing_drop_rate (rate-limited typing events), edit_conflict_rate. Logs: every PATCH with old/new version and userId for SOC2 audit trail. Traces: span from HTTP POST /messages through INSERT → NOTIFY → Redis pub → Socket.IO emit. Alerts: pg_listen_lag > 1s (notifier stuck), msg_publish_to_deliver p99 > 500ms, notifier process crash count > 0.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior approach: socket.emit('send', msg) → io.to(channel).emit('msg', msg). Breaks 4 ways: (1) no persistence, msg lost on server restart between emit and any persistence call; (2) typing indicator with no throttle = 100 msgs/sec from one user holding shift; (3) multi-tab read receipts go backwards because last write wins not max wins; (4) edit history not preserved — compliance violation. The killer bug: at 5k viewers, broadcasting on every keystroke as 'typing' melts the websocket bus and the actual message events get delayed 30s behind.

title: Live revenue dashboard with multi-chart sync, drill-down, and historical replay
business_context: Fintech ops team watches a live revenue dashboard during product launches. 8 charts (revenue/min, top SKUs, geo heatmap, error rate, etc.) must stay synchronized — drilling into '2:15pm spike' must cross-filter all charts. 50 concurrent viewers per dashboard; latest data within 2s of transaction commit. Historical replay lets execs review yesterday's launch by scrubbing a time slider. If this fails: launch decisions are made on stale data, finance loses confidence in observability.
constraints: Sub-2s latency from order commit to dashboard pixel
Each viewer can apply filters server-side (don't ship all data)
Replay mode: scrub 24h timeline at 60fps without re-querying every tick
Server crash must not lose pending aggregations
Multi-chart cross-filter state shared per viewer session
failure_modes_to_handle: Change-stream resume token loss after Mongo failover
Viewer applies filter that returns 1M rows
Replay scrub jitter overwhelms backend with queries
Two viewers in same session drift out of sync
architecture_reasoning:
1. Orders write to Postgres (source of truth, ACID for money). Mongo holds rollup_minute time-series collection populated by a worker tailing Postgres logical replication (wal2json) — separating OLTP from analytics fan-out.
2. Live mode: Mongo change stream on rollup_minute → Socket.IO worker → fan-out to viewers in dash:{dashId} room with viewer-specific filter applied server-side.
3. Bottleneck #1: filter evaluation per viewer per event. Solution: compile filter to a predicate once on viewer subscribe, run on every event. Bottleneck #2: replay scrubbing — pre-aggregate 5-minute buckets in Mongo, scrub queries hit pre-agg not raw.
4. Hybrid DB: Postgres for transactional truth (orders, refunds with FK), Mongo for time-series rollups (change stream is the killer feature here — no need to poll). Doing this with Postgres alone means LISTEN/NOTIFY + 50 per-viewer LISTENs = pg backend exhaustion.
5. Redis adapter for Socket.IO multi-node. Change-stream worker is singleton-with-leader-election (Redlock) — only one node tails the stream and republishes via Redis to other nodes.
6. Recovery: resume token persisted to Redis with TTL 1h; on worker restart, resumeAfter that token; if expired, full-resync from last_seen_ts in Postgres.
7. Rejected: Kafka — overkill for this scale and adds operational burden. Rejected per-chart sockets — multiplies connection count 8x; one socket multiplexes via event types.

database_design.indexes: Postgres orders: BRIN on (ts) for time-range scans, much smaller than B-tree at this volume. Mongo time-series: implicit metaField+ts index plus the explicit dim.sku+ts and dim.country+ts for filter dimensions.
database_design.queries: Live event delivery: change stream pipeline [{$match:{operationType:{$in:['insert','update']}}}, {$project:{fullDocument:1, _id:0}}]. Replay scrub: db.rollup_5m.find({ts:{$gte:t0,$lte:t1}}).sort({ts:1}) — covered by time-series clustered index. Filter eval is in-process JS, no query — the change stream document is the input.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Postgres: standard tx for order insert. Rollup writer worker uses Mongo session for atomic upsert of rollup bucket: db.rollup_minute.findOneAndUpdate({bucket,dim},{$inc:{revenueCents,orderCount}},{upsert:true,session}) inside a transaction so a crash mid-batch doesn't double-count. Idempotency via processed_lsn table in Postgres updated in same logical txn boundary.

socket_io_design.namespaces: /dashboards isolated namespace — auth scope is org-level, different from /docs or /chat.
socket_io_design.rooms: dash:{dashId} for all viewers of a dashboard. NO per-viewer rooms — viewer-specific filtering is done in handler before emit, but we use socket.emit directly to each viewer when filters diverge significantly (more on this in handler).
socket_io_design.events: type LiveTick = { ts:number; metric:string; value:number; dim:Record<string,string> }; type ReplayChunk = { ts0:number; ts1:number; series:Array<{metric:string;points:[number,number][]}> }; Events: 'dash:subscribe' (C→S {dashId, filter}, ack with initial state), 'dash:filter' (C→S update filter), 'tick' (S→C single viewer), 'replay:request' (C→S {t0,t1}, ack with chunk), 'mode:switch' (C→S 'live'|'replay').
socket_io_design.middleware_order: jwt-auth → org-scope → dashboard-read-permission → filter-validation (reject filters that estimate > 100k rows via pre-computed cardinality table) → rate-limit (10 filter-changes/min/user)
socket_io_design.adapter_choice: redis — required for multi-node. Change-stream worker holds the singleton lease via Redlock; on lease loss it shuts down its stream cleanly and another worker resumes from the persisted token.

race_conditions_addressed: Filter change arrives while tick is in flight: filterRef + server-side predicate update means worst case is one extra tick with old filter, harmless because client also re-evaluates against current filter (defense in depth).

Change-stream worker lease expires mid-batch: in-transaction LSN update means crash leaves processed_lsn at last committed batch; new leader resumes cleanly. Without redlock auto-extend → lease loss → process.exit prevents split-brain dual-writers.
Replay request flood from scrub: rate-limit on replay:request (server) + client-side debounce on scrub (200ms) caps Mongo query load.

Snapshot/live overlap on subscribe: server returns snapshot of last 15min, then live ticks include events potentially within that window — client dedups by (ts, sku, country) tuple or accepts duplicate aggregation since rollups are idempotent upserts.
scaling_considerations: 10x (500 viewers, 50 dashboards, 10k orders/sec): rollup tailer becomes bottleneck — partition by sku_id hash, run N tailer shards each with its own LSN. Per-viewer filter eval at 50k tickers × 500 viewers = 25M filter-evals/sec → CPU-bound; introduce filter aggregation (group viewers with identical filters, eval once). 100x: change-stream + tailer can't keep up — switch to push-based via Postgres logical replication slot consumed by purpose-built consumer (pglogical or Debezium → Kafka).
observability: Metrics: tailer_lag_ms (gap between order.ts and rollup write), tick_fan_out_ms (per emit), filter_eval_us histogram, replay_query_ms, viewer_count per dashboard. Logs: filter changes per user (audit + abuse detection), lease acquire/lose events. Traces: order INSERT → tailer pickup → Mongo upsert → Redis pub → socket emit → client receive. Alert: tailer_lag > 5s, lease loss > 1/hour (Redis instability), filter_eval p99 > 1ms (filter has runaway cardinality).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior approach: setInterval(()=>io.emit(await db.aggregate()), 1000). Breaks: (1) every viewer pays full aggregation cost per second, n² explosion; (2) no resume, server restart = lost ticks during outage; (3) viewer-specific filters force per-viewer queries; (4) replay = re-query for every scrub frame, melts Mongo. The killer bug: at 20 viewers, the aggregation pipeline starts taking 3s but the interval is 1s → queries pile up → Mongo connection pool exhausts → entire app freezes.

title: Authoritative server for 2D arena with client prediction, lag compensation, and interest management
business_context: Top-down arena game, 20 players per match, 30 ticks/sec server simulation. Players see other players within 800px radius (interest management). Server is authoritative — clients predict locally but reconcile to server state. Reconnection mid-match must restore game state without giving advantage. Scale: 5000 concurrent matches across the platform. If naive: clients diverge → cheaters win → game dies in a month.
constraints: 30Hz server tick, max 50ms p99 server→client latency
Interest culling: send only entities within 800px to each player
Reconnect within 30s restores into running match
No client can desync from server for more than 200ms
Anti-cheat: server rejects movements exceeding maxSpeed × dt
failure_modes_to_handle: Lagging player teleporting (rubber-banding) on reconciliation
Server tick falling behind real-time under load
Cheater sending hand-crafted inputs at 1000Hz
Reconnecting player rejoins after match ended
Player disconnect mid-action (in air, reloading)
architecture_reasoning:
1. Each match is a deterministic simulation on one node (sticky routing by matchId hash). Inputs flow client→server tagged with clientTick. Server applies inputs at next tick, broadcasts diff (delta from prior snapshot) to each player in their interest set.
2. Client prediction: client applies its own input immediately to a predicted state. On server snapshot arrival, client reconciles: re-apply all inputs newer than the acknowledged tick on top of server state. If divergence < threshold, smooth-blend; else snap.
3. Bottleneck: per-player interest filtering at 30Hz × 20 players × 100 entities = 60k checks/sec/match × 5000 matches = 300M/sec aggregate. Solution: spatial hash grid per match, O(k) neighbor queries; precompute interest set per player per tick.
4. Postgres chosen: match metadata, player stats, match results are transactional and joined. Game state itself lives in Node.js memory (in-process) — persisting per-tick is infeasible. We persist match snapshots every 5s as a recovery checkpoint and append-only input log for replay/anti-cheat review.
5. Adapter: no Redis adapter needed per match because all 20 players sticky-route to one node. Cross-node coordination is only for matchmaking + match-end persistence. Redis is used for matchmaking queue (BullMQ) and short-TTL state (live match index).
6. Recovery: on disconnect, server keeps player slot active for 30s, queues their inputs as 'no-op'. On reconnect to same node, replay last N ticks of snapshots to client + resume.
7. Rejected: per-player rooms — sticky routing means we never broadcast to a room, we direct-emit per player with their interest snapshot. Rejected event sourcing on Mongo with change stream — per-tick latency would be 50ms+ from change-stream propagation alone.

database_design.schema:
CREATE TABLE matches (
  id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  status TEXT NOT NULL CHECK (status IN ('lobby','running','ended')),
  node_id TEXT,
  started_at TIMESTAMPTZ, ended_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
  result JSONB
);

CREATE TABLE match_players (
  match_id BIGINT REFERENCES matches(id),
  user_id BIGINT NOT NULL,
  slot INT NOT NULL,
  joined_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  left_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
  reconnect_token TEXT NOT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (match_id, user_id)
);

CREATE TABLE match_snapshots (
  match_id BIGINT REFERENCES matches(id),
  tick BIGINT NOT NULL,
  ts TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  state BYTEA NOT NULL, -- msgpack-encoded
  PRIMARY KEY (match_id, tick)
);

CREATE TABLE match_inputs (
  match_id BIGINT REFERENCES matches(id),
  user_id BIGINT NOT NULL,
  tick BIGINT NOT NULL,
  client_tick BIGINT NOT NULL,
  input JSONB NOT NULL,
  ts TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  PRIMARY KEY (match_id, user_id, tick)
);
database_design.indexes: match_snapshots PK (match_id, tick) — fetch latest N. match_inputs PK supports anti-cheat replay queries. matches partial index on (status) WHERE status='running' for active match scan.
database_design.queries: Recovery: SELECT state FROM match_snapshots WHERE match_id=$1 ORDER BY tick DESC LIMIT 1 + SELECT input FROM match_inputs WHERE match_id=$1 AND tick > $2 ORDER BY tick. Anti-cheat review: SELECT input FROM match_inputs WHERE match_id=$1 AND user_id=$2 ORDER BY tick — full input replay against server-recomputed state.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Match end: tx that UPDATEs matches.status='ended', INSERTs result, updates player stats, and DELETEs old snapshots — all-or-nothing for stats integrity. Snapshot writes: no tx needed (idempotent on PK). Match start: SELECT pg_try_advisory_xact_lock(match_id) prevents two nodes claiming same match during failover.

socket_io_design.namespaces: /game — separate from /lobby because game has much stricter rate-limits and binary payloads.
socket_io_design.rooms: match:{matchId} — used only for non-tick events (chat, end). Tick broadcasts use socket.emit per player due to interest culling.
socket_io_design.events: type Input = { clientTick:number; seq:number; mv:[number,number]; fire?:boolean }; type Snapshot = { tick:number; ackedInputSeq:number; entities:Array<{id:string;x:number;y:number;hp:number;type:string}> }; Events: 'input' (C→S, no ack — fire-and-forget), 'snapshot' (S→C per player), 'match:joined' (S→C with reconnectToken + tick), 'match:ended' (S→C).
socket_io_design.middleware_order: jwt-auth → match-membership-check → input-rate-limit (60Hz max, 2x server tick) → input-validation (movement vector magnitude, fire cooldown)
socket_io_design.adapter_choice: none for in-match (sticky routing). Redis adapter for /lobby cross-node matchmaking events. Game nodes hold socket affinity via cookie/sticky session at LB.

race_conditions_addressed: Input out-of-order arrival: server tracks lastInputSeq per player, rejects inputs with seq <= lastInputSeq.
Reconnect during tick: detach() leaves slot intact with disconnectAt timestamp; tick loop reaps after 30s. Inputs from old socket dropped because socket reference replaced atomically in reattach.

Cheater sends 1000 inputs/sec: server-side input-rate-limit middleware drops >60/sec; magnitude check rejects vectors > 1.01 unit. Per-input lastInputSeq check is single-threaded inside tick so no race.

Snapshot persistence concurrent with tick mutation: persistSnapshot reads from in-memory Map; JS single-threaded so no torn read, but we encode synchronously then await INSERT — safe because mutation only happens in tick handler which is also single-threaded.
Node failover mid-match: Postgres advisory lock on matchId prevents two nodes claiming same match; new node restores from last snapshot + replays inputs since snapshot.tick.
scaling_considerations: 10x (50k matches): single node handles ~200 matches at 30Hz, so 250 game nodes needed. Sticky routing at LB layer via matchId hash. Postgres write rate (snapshots + inputs) becomes ~5k writes/sec — partition match_snapshots and match_inputs by matchId range. 100x: per-node CPU bound by interest culling — switch to spatial hash grid (O(k) instead of O(n²) neighbor queries) and SIMD-friendly entity layout.
observability: Metrics: tick_duration_ms (alarm if > TICK_MS), input_drop_rate (rate-limited inputs), snapshot_size_bytes, reconcile_divergence (server tick - client ack), match_count gauge per node. Logs: match start/end with player roster, reconnects, anti-cheat rejections. Traces: per-tick span at debug level (sampled). Alerts: tick falls behind real-time by > 1 tick for 10s (CPU saturated), reconcile_divergence p99 > 200ms (network bad), snapshot_persist_ms > 100 (DB slow).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior approach: socket.on('move', pos => { player.pos = pos; io.to(match).emit('state', allPlayers); }). Breaks: (1) client is authoritative = trivially cheatable; (2) full state broadcast at 30Hz × 20 players = 600 emits/sec/match × 5000 = 3M emits/sec; (3) no interest mgmt = bandwidth scales O(n²) within match; (4) reconnect = ¯\_(ツ)_/¯. The killer bug: at 100 concurrent matches on one node, broadcast loop blocks event loop for 200ms+ → all sockets get backpressured → snowball to ALB timeout → all matches die simultaneously.

title: Live auction bid sync with optimistic UI, rollback, and tamper-proof audit log
business_context: Sothebys-style live online auction. 200 concurrent bidders per lot, $1M+ items. Bids must serialize correctly (last accepted bid wins) and propagate to all viewers within 250ms. Optimistic UI must roll back gracefully when bid is outbid. Audit log must be tamper-evident (hash-chained) for regulatory disputes. If this fails: $$$ legal liability, regulator pulls license.
constraints: Bid serialization: no two bids accepted at same price
Audit log immutable + tamper-evident (hash chain)
Outbid notification within 250ms
Optimistic UI rollback must feel smooth not jarring
Replay attack prevention (same signed bid submitted twice)
failure_modes_to_handle: Two bidders submit same bid at same millisecond
Client retries bid after network blip, server already accepted
Server crashes between bid acceptance and broadcast
Bidder disconnects holding the leading bid
Malicious client forges bid client-side
architecture_reasoning:
1. Bid flow: client builds bid (lotId, amount, clientNonce, signature) → POST /bids (HTTP, durable) → server validates → SELECT pg_advisory_xact_lock(lotId) inside tx → check current_bid < new_amount → INSERT bid → UPDATE lot.current_bid → trigger appends to audit_log with hash(prev_hash || row) → commit → pg_notify('lot:{id}') → fan-out via Socket.IO.
2. Advisory lock is per-lot, so different lots bid concurrently without contention. Inside the lock, comparison + insert is serialized — no race for top bid.
3. Bottleneck: hot lot contention. 200 bidders smashing 'bid' at end-of-auction → 200 serialized advisory-lock acquisitions → throughput ~500 bids/sec per lot. Acceptable for auction semantics (humans don't bid faster than that anyway).
4. Postgres: ACID required for money. Hash-chained audit log via trigger that reads prev row's hash inside same tx — only Postgres gives us strong-enough isolation (REPEATABLE READ + advisory lock) to make this safe.
5. Adapter: Redis adapter. pg_notify → listener worker → Redis pub/sub → all socket nodes broadcast to lot:{id} room.
6. Replay protection: client nonce stored UNIQUE per (user, lot). Retry with same nonce is idempotent.
7. Rejected: optimistic concurrency without advisory lock — SERIALIZABLE isolation would also work but with retry storms at high contention; advisory lock is more predictable. Rejected event sourcing with append-only — we want current_bid materialized for O(1) read.

database_design.indexes: lots PK + status partial index for live-lot listing. bids (lot_id, created_at DESC) for history; UNIQUE (lot_id, bidder_id, client_nonce) for idempotency. audit_log (lot_id, id) for chain verification scan.
database_design.queries: Place bid (in tx): SELECT pg_advisory_xact_lock($1); SELECT current_bid_cents, ends_at FROM lots WHERE id=$1 FOR UPDATE; — then conditional INSERT+UPDATE+append_audit. Read top bid: SELECT current_bid_cents, current_bidder_id FROM lots WHERE id=$1 — O(1). Verify chain: SELECT id, prev_hash, row_hash, payload FROM audit_log WHERE lot_id=$1 ORDER BY id — recompute hashes client-side or in audit script.
database_design.transaction_strategy: REPEATABLE READ isolation + pg_advisory_xact_lock(lotId). Tx body: lock → SELECT lot FOR UPDATE → validate (amount > current + min_increment, within auction window) → INSERT bids (catch UNIQUE = idempotent return existing) → UPDATE lots → SELECT append_audit() → COMMIT. Failure modes: validation fail → INSERT bid with status='rejected', append audit, COMMIT (still record attempt). Trigger fires NOTIFY only on actual current_bid_cents change.

socket_io_design.namespaces: /auction — bidders + viewers connect here. Separate from /admin (auctioneer controls).
socket_io_design.rooms: lot:{lotId} for bid updates. lot:{lotId}:status for sale state. Joined via 'lot:watch' event.
socket_io_design.events: type BidUpdate = { lotId:string; currentBidCents:number; currentBidderId:string; bidCount:number; ts:number }; type BidAccepted = { bidId:string; lotId:string; amountCents:number; nonce:string }; type BidRejected = { lotId:string; nonce:string; reason:'outbid'|'too_low'|'closed'|'invalid_sig' }; Events: 'lot:watch' (C→S), 'bid:update' (S→C broadcast), 'bid:accepted' (S→C single bidder ack via room of one), 'bid:rejected' (S→C single bidder). Note: bid SUBMISSION is HTTP POST, not socket — socket is fan-out only.
socket_io_design.middleware_order: jwt-auth → kyc-check (bidder must be verified) → rate-limit (5 bids/sec/user) → audit
socket_io_design.adapter_choice: redis — multi-node mandatory. pg_notify worker (separate process) consumes notifications and republishes via Redis to all nodes.

race_conditions_addressed: Two bidders submit at same instant: pg_advisory_xact_lock serializes; second sees updated current_bid in FOR UPDATE row and is rejected with 'too_low'.
Retry after network blip: UNIQUE (lot_id, bidder_id, client_nonce) → server returns existing bid status, no double-charge or double-counting.
Server crash mid-tx: tx rolls back atomically; client retries with same nonce → idempotent. Audit log only contains committed entries.
Outbid notification race: client tracks isMyLead from server broadcast; pending bid auto-rolls-back when broadcast amount exceeds it and bidder isn't self.
Hash-chain tampering: any insertion/deletion in audit_log breaks the chain since each row hashes prior row's hash; off-line verification job recomputes and alerts on mismatch.
scaling_considerations: 10x (2000 bidders/lot): bid throughput still limited by per-lot advisory lock to ~500/sec — acceptable, this is the auction semantic. Fan-out scales linearly with viewers — Redis adapter handles. 100x (multiple thousand concurrent live lots): pg_notify becomes a single-process bottleneck on the listener — run N listeners each LISTENing to a hash partition of lot IDs.
observability: Metrics: bid_accept_ms histogram, bid_reject_count by reason, lock_wait_ms (lots stuck in contention), audit_chain_lag (gap between bid commit and audit row). Logs: every bid attempt with userId, lotId, amount, signature, decision, reject_reason — structured for forensics. Traces: span from HTTP POST through tx → NOTIFY → Redis pub → socket emit. Alerts: chain verification job mismatch (corruption!), bid_accept_ms p99 > 500ms, NOTIFY listener disconnect.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: socket.on('bid', b => { if (b.amount > lot.current) { lot.current = b.amount; io.emit('bid', b); } }). Breaks: (1) no persistence, server restart loses bids; (2) check-then-act race on lot.current = double winners at same price; (3) no signature = clients forge bids; (4) no audit = no defense in regulator dispute; (5) optimistic UI without rollback path = users think they won when they didn't = legal nightmare. Killer bug: at auction-end frenzy with 200 bidders, two bids arriving 5ms apart get accepted at same price because the JS read-update isn't atomic.

title: Multi-device presence with idle detection and scoped visibility
business_context: Team-collab product needs accurate 'who is online and where' visualization. User on phone + laptop + tablet = ONE green dot. Idle after 5min no input. Presence is scoped: I see coworkers only if we share a workspace. Scale: 200k DAU, peak 30k concurrent. If wrong: privacy leak (showing offline users as online to ex-coworkers), or 3 green dots for the same person which looks broken.
constraints: One user across N devices = one presence record
Idle transition after 5min inactivity, online again on any input
Presence scope: visible only to users sharing a workspace
Disconnect must not flicker offline-online-offline
Cross-node fan-out — user A on node-1 sees user B's presence change on node-2
failure_modes_to_handle: Two tabs of same user, one disconnects (should stay online)
Server crash mid-presence-update
Workspace membership change while user is online
Heartbeat lost due to network blip but user actually online
architecture_reasoning:
1. Presence is ephemeral state — lives in Redis (hash per user with deviceId→{lastSeen, status}). Mongo holds workspace memberships (durable, scope source-of-truth). Hybrid is the right split: don't persist presence to disk (write storm), don't put workspace memberships in Redis (need to query).
2. Heartbeat: client emits 'hb' every 20s; server SETs presence:{userId}:{deviceId} with TTL 60s. On connect, also publishes 'presence:change' to Redis pub/sub. On disconnect, decrements device count but doesn't immediately set offline — waits 30s grace then publishes if last device went away.
3. Fan-out: presence:change pub/sub message contains userId + new status. Each node looks up workspaces containing userId (Mongo) and emits to ws:{workspaceId} rooms it has sockets in. This avoids broadcasting every presence change to every node's every socket.
4. Idle: client tracks input events, sends 'idle' or 'active' on transition. Server stores idleSince in presence hash. Subscribers see idle separately from offline.
5. Mongo for workspaces (transactional membership changes, joins with user profile), Redis for presence (TTL, ephemeral). Postgres alternative for workspaces would also work, but Mongo's denormalized membership document is faster to query for 'workspaces containing user X'.
6. Rejected: storing presence in Mongo with TTL index — TTL is eventual (60s+ lag), too imprecise. Rejected per-user socket subscription — quadratic relationship explosion.

database_design.indexes: Mongo Workspace.memberIds — indexed array for reverse lookup. Redis: no indexes, key-based access.
database_design.queries: Workspaces for user: db.workspaces.find({memberIds:userId},{_id:1,memberIds:1}). Presence read: HGETALL presence:{userId}. Bulk presence for workspace: pipeline of HGETALL for each memberId. Members in workspace: workspace.memberIds (already loaded).
database_design.transaction_strategy: Workspace membership add/remove: Mongo tx wrapping the $addToSet/$pull on workspace.memberIds. On commit, publish 'membership:change' so connected user sees workspace appear/disappear in their roster and presence subscriptions retarget.

socket_io_design.namespaces: /presence — separate from /chat etc. Heartbeats are noisy; separate handler keeps them off the main event loop.
socket_io_design.rooms: ws:{workspaceId} — sockets join workspaces they're members of. Presence updates fan out only to these rooms.
socket_io_design.events: type PresenceState = { userId:string; status:'online'|'idle'|'offline'; lastSeen:number; idleSince?:number }; Events: 'hb' (C→S every 20s, no ack), 'idle' (C→S), 'active' (C→S), 'presence:roster' (S→C on subscribe), 'presence:change' (S→C broadcast), 'ws:subscribe' (C→S list of workspaceIds, ack with initial roster).
socket_io_design.middleware_order: jwt-auth → device-id-extract (from handshake header) → connection-cap (max 10 devices/user)
socket_io_design.adapter_choice: redis — multi-node. Internal pub/sub channel 'presence:change' separate from Socket.IO adapter so we can filter per-node before re-broadcasting.

race_conditions_addressed: Tab close + tab open in quick succession (refresh): grace timer is cancelled on connect; no offline flicker.
Heartbeat lost: TTL 60s with 20s heartbeat allows 2 missed beats before expiry; if connection still alive, next hb refreshes.
Two devices: HDEL one device leaves hash non-empty → no offline publish. Only when hlen==0 does grace timer arm.
Workspace membership change mid-session: separate 'membership:change' event prompts client to re-subscribe with updated workspaceIds; old ws room continues until socket leaves.
scaling_considerations: 10x (300k concurrent): Redis pub/sub fan-out of presence:change becomes hot — every change goes to every node. Move to sharded pub/sub by workspaceId range. Mongo Workspace.find on every presence change is a query/sec storm — cache memberIds in Redis with invalidation on membership:change. 100x: heartbeat traffic dominates — increase interval to 60s with longer TTL, accept coarser idle detection.
observability: Metrics: presence_devices_per_user histogram, hb_rate, idle_transition_rate, presence_fanout_emit_count per second per node. Logs: connection/disconnect with userId, deviceId. Alerts: presence_devices_per_user p99 > 5 (account compromise?), presence:change pub/sub backlog > 1k.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: socket.on('connect', () => io.emit('online', userId)). Breaks: (1) two tabs = two 'online' then one 'offline' on tab close = user shows offline despite being online; (2) no scope = whole org sees you; (3) no idle distinction; (4) heartbeat-less = stale 'online' on crash. Killer bug: user closes laptop → tcp keepalive eventually fires 2h later → 'offline' broadcast 2h after the fact.

title: PDF annotations with position-relative-to-page and per-user permissions
business_context: Legal/healthcare doc-review needs multi-user PDF annotation: highlights, comments, drawings. Annotations must stay glued to text even after PDF re-render at different zoom. Real-time: I see your highlight appear within 200ms. Permissions: viewer can only see annotations from their org. Scale: 50k docs, 20 simul reviewers per hot doc. If broken: lawyers miss critical comments → malpractice.
constraints: Annotation position survives PDF re-render at any zoom
Permission: per-annotation visibility (private/team/public)
Real-time append/edit/delete with optimistic UI
Soft delete with 30-day undo retention
Coordinated viewport sync (optional: 'follow user') without forcing
failure_modes_to_handle: Two reviewers comment on same selection simultaneously
Annotation position drifts on PDF reflow
Permission change mid-session leaves stale annotation visible
Reviewer joins doc with 10k existing annotations (pagination)
architecture_reasoning:
1. Position model: store anchor as { pageIndex, quadPoints: [x0,y0,x1,y1,...] } in normalized [0,1] coords relative to PDF page bbox. Client multiplies by current page render dimensions — survives zoom.
2. Mongo chosen: annotations are document-shaped (varied schema per type: highlight has quadPoints, comment has text, drawing has stroke path). Discriminators model this cleanly. Change streams give us real-time without polling.
3. Fan-out: change stream → Socket.IO worker → per-doc room. But permission filtering must happen at fan-out time: server checks annotation.visibility against viewer's orgId/teamId before emit.
4. Bottleneck: change stream is per-database (or per-collection in 4.0+). At 50k docs with low per-doc write rate, single change stream cursor is fine; we filter by docId in pipeline.
5. Adapter: Redis adapter. Per-doc room. Permission filter is per-socket so we use socket.emit individually after iterating room members.
6. Recovery: client tracks lastUpdatedAt; on reconnect, GET /docs/{id}/annotations?since=lastUpdatedAt to catch up.
7. Rejected: per-page rooms — too granular, churn from page-flipping users. Rejected pixel-coords — break on zoom.

database_design.indexes: (docId, pageIndex, deletedAt) for page-scoped fetch excluding deleted. (docId, updatedAt) for since-based catch-up. (authorId) for 'my annotations' view. TTL on deletedAt (30 days) — partial filter expression to skip undeleted: { partialFilterExpression: { deletedAt: { $exists: true } }, expireAfterSeconds: 30*86400 }.
database_design.queries: Page fetch: db.annotations.find({docId, pageIndex, deletedAt: null}).sort({createdAt:1}). Catch-up: db.annotations.find({docId, updatedAt: {$gt: since}}).sort({updatedAt:1}).limit(500). Change stream: pipeline [{$match:{'fullDocument.docId':{$in:activeDocIds}}}, {$project:{fullDocument:1, operationType:1, documentKey:1}}].
database_design.transaction_strategy: Single-doc operations don't need tx. Comment-with-reply uses tx: load annotation FOR UPDATE → push reply → version++ → commit. Permission change (visibility) is single update, no tx; concurrent edit and visibility change handled by client refetch on conflict (version check).

socket_io_design.namespaces: /annotations — separate so PDF doc namespace can scale differently.
socket_io_design.rooms: doc:{docId} per document. No per-page subroom; permission filter happens server-side per emit.
socket_io_design.events: type AnnoEvt = { type:'created'|'updated'|'deleted'; annotation: Annotation }; Events: 'doc:open' (C→S, ack with paginated initial annotations), 'doc:sync' (C→S since timestamp), 'annotation:create' (C→S with ack returning canonical record), 'annotation:update' (C→S), 'annotation:delete' (C→S), 'annotation:change' (S→C broadcast, permission-filtered).
socket_io_design.adapter_choice: redis. Change stream worker is per-process subscribed to its share via consistent hash; republishes via Redis internal channel for inter-node fan-out.

race_conditions_addressed: Concurrent comment on same anchor: each is a separate Annotation document, both succeed; client renders both side-by-side.
Concurrent edit of same annotation: version check in findOneAndUpdate returns null on stale version; client gets conflict, refetches canonical from change stream broadcast.

Permission change while annotation visible: server filters at fan-out, but client may have stale visible state. Add periodic resync (every 60s call doc:sync) for hardening. Better: on org/team membership change broadcast 'membership:change' to user's all sockets prompting a full re-fetch.
Optimistic create echoed back from change stream: ack returns canonical record with server-generated _id; client uses ack-returned _id directly so no temp-id reconciliation needed.
scaling_considerations: 10x (200 reviewers/doc): per-emit canSeeAnnotation × room size becomes expensive — pre-group sockets in room by (orgId, teamIds tuple) and emit per group. 100x (500k docs): single change stream cursor saturates — shard the watch with $match on docId hash and run N workers.
observability: Metrics: anno_create_to_render_ms, change_stream_lag (now - change.clusterTime), annotations_per_doc histogram, permission_filter_skips_per_sec. Logs: every visibility change with old/new + actor for compliance. Alerts: change_stream_lag > 2s (cursor stalled), resume_token loss (had to full-resync).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: store pixel coords + io.to(doc).emit. Breaks: (1) zoom → annotations drift off text; (2) no permission filter → confidential client A annotations leak to org B reviewers on same shared doc; (3) no change stream → if you persist async after emit, server crash loses annotation; if you emit after persist with two callers, ordering can flip. Killer bug: at 10k existing annotations, initial load times out, client retries, retries, retries → DDoS on Mongo.

title: Push notifications with offline fallback, grouping, and cross-device read-sync
business_context: Productivity app needs notifications for @mentions, assignments, comments. If user connected → push via socket (instant). If offline → queue + email after 5min. 10 likes on same post should collapse to one notification. Read on phone marks read on desktop. Scale: 5M users, 50k notifications/min peak.
constraints: Push instantly to connected sessions
Fall back to email/SMS if no session within 5min
Group N notifications about same target into one (rolling 5min window)
Read state syncs across user's devices within 1s
Notification history persists for 90 days, searchable
failure_modes_to_handle: User connects after notification dispatched — must still receive in-app
Email and in-app both fired (duplicate)
Read marked offline then user comes back online — must sync to all devices
Notification arrives while user has 0 sessions but appears mid-dispatch
architecture_reasoning:
1. Notification create: API call → INSERT into Postgres notifications (durable, with idempotency key) → enqueue BullMQ job 'deliver:{id}'. Job checks Redis for active sessions; if any, emit via Socket.IO and mark in_app_delivered_at; if none, wait 5min and trigger email branch.
2. Grouping: before insert, check Mongo notification_drafts collection for a draft with same (userId, targetType, targetId) opened within last 5min; if exists, append actor + count++; else create. Drafts flush to notifications after 5min idle or 10 actors. Mongo chosen for drafts (mutable, varied actor arrays). Postgres for delivered notifications (audit, joinable).
3. Read sync: read:mark event → UPDATE notifications SET read_at = LEAST(now(), read_at) where id=$1 AND user_id=$2 → broadcast 'notif:read' to user:{userId} room (all their devices).
4. Bottleneck: at 50k/min = ~830/sec, BullMQ handles easily. Email fallback timer is the tricky bit — using BullMQ delayed job, cancelled if delivered in-app first.
5. Hybrid DB because drafts are document-shaped + mutable (Mongo wins), delivered notifications are transactional + reportable (Postgres wins).
6. Recovery: client tracks last_seen_id; on connect, GET /notifications?since=lastSeenId — receives any missed in-app pushes.

database_design.schema:
-- Postgres notifications
CREATE TABLE notifications (
  id BIGSERIAL PK,
  user_id BIGINT NOT NULL,
  idempotency_key TEXT NOT NULL,
  type TEXT NOT NULL,
  target_type TEXT, target_id TEXT,
  actor_ids BIGINT[] NOT NULL,
  actor_count INT NOT NULL,
  body TEXT NOT NULL,
  in_app_delivered_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
  email_dispatched_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
  read_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
  created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  UNIQUE (user_id, idempotency_key)
);
CREATE INDEX ON notifications (user_id, id DESC) WHERE read_at IS NULL;
CREATE INDEX ON notifications (user_id, created_at DESC);

// Mongo: notification_drafts
const DraftSchema = new Schema({
  userId: { type: String, required: true },
  targetType: String,
  targetId: String,
  type: String,
  actorIds: [String],
  body: String,
  firstAt: { type: Date, default: Date.now },
  lastAt: { type: Date, default: Date.now, index: true },
  flushedAt: Date,
});
DraftSchema.index({ userId: 1, targetType: 1, targetId: 1, flushedAt: 1 }, { partialFilterExpression: { flushedAt: { $exists: false } } });
DraftSchema.index({ lastAt: 1 }); // for flush scan
database_design.indexes: notifications (user_id, id DESC) WHERE read_at IS NULL — unread badge query, hot. (user_id, created_at DESC) — history list. UNIQUE (user_id, idempotency_key) for dedup. Drafts: partial index on (userId, target, !flushed) for grouping lookup; (lastAt) for flush-scan worker.
database_design.queries: Unread count: SELECT count(*) FROM notifications WHERE user_id=$1 AND read_at IS NULL LIMIT 100. History page: SELECT ... ORDER BY id DESC WHERE user_id=$1 AND id < $cursor. Catch-up: SELECT * WHERE user_id=$1 AND id > $lastSeen ORDER BY id. Draft lookup: db.drafts.findOneAndUpdate({userId,targetType,targetId,flushedAt:{$exists:false}}, {$addToSet:{actorIds:$actor}, $set:{lastAt:now}}, {upsert:true,returnDocument:'after'}).
database_design.transaction_strategy: Notification insert from draft flush: Mongo tx around (draft.flushedAt=now AND INSERT into pg.notifications). To keep Mongo+pg consistent without 2PC, use outbox: write flushedAt=now in Mongo with delivered=false, then enqueue BullMQ job that does the pg insert and updates draft.delivered=true. Idempotency_key=draft._id makes the pg insert idempotent.

socket_io_design.rooms: user:{userId} — every device of a user joins same room.
socket_io_design.events: type Notif = { id:string; type:string; body:string; createdAt:string; actors:string[]; readAt?:string }; Events: 'subscribe' (C→S ack with initial unread + lastId), 'notif:new' (S→C), 'notif:read' (S→C broadcast; also C→S to mark), 'notif:read-all' (C→S).
socket_io_design.adapter_choice: redis. user:{userId} room may span multiple nodes → adapter handles cross-node fan-out.

race_conditions_addressed: Notif dispatched while user connecting: emit goes to room with 0 sockets → email branch scheduled. When client connects, 'subscribe' with sinceId pulls the missed notif from DB. Email later fires and finds in_app_delivered_at still null — but it's already been seen via subscribe. Mitigation: subscribe handler updates in_app_delivered_at for fetched unread notifs.
Email + in-app double: email worker SELECTs FOR UPDATE and rechecks in_app_delivered_at + email_dispatched_at before sending — atomic skip if either is set.
Read on phone while desktop offline: UPDATE WHERE read_at IS NULL means second device's mark-read is no-op; broadcast tells desktop on reconnect (via subscribe pulling current state).

Draft flush race: two flush jobs for same draftId — Mongo findOneAndUpdate with flushedAt:{$exists:false} returns null for second worker → no-op. PG INSERT ON CONFLICT (user_id, idempotency_key) prevents double-insert.
scaling_considerations: 10x (500k notif/min): BullMQ deliver worker pool scales horizontally; pg notifications table partitioning by created_at month becomes important at 500M rows. 100x: per-user fan-out concentration (active users with 100s of devices) hot-spots Redis adapter — separate notification room sharding by userId hash range.
observability: Metrics: notif_create_to_emit_ms, draft_group_size histogram, email_fallback_rate, read_sync_latency. Logs: every dispatch with notifId, channel (in_app|email), userId, sessions count. Alerts: deliver queue backlog > 10k, email-fallback rate > 30% (sockets dropping?), draft flush age > 10min (worker stuck).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: on event, io.to(user).emit('notif',data) and INSERT into notifications. Breaks: (1) no grouping → 10 likes = 10 toasts, user mutes app; (2) offline user = lost notif (room emit goes nowhere); (3) email fallback firing in parallel = duplicate notification; (4) read state local-only = checking phone leaves desktop badge red forever. Killer bug: at scale, 'like' bursts (1000 likes/min on viral post) create 1000 in-flight Socket.IO emits all at once → backpressure on adapter Redis pub/sub → 10s lag → users see notifications for events that already happened.

title: 300 device telemetry at 10Hz with downsampling, anomaly alerts, and per-viewer subscription
business_context: Industrial monitoring: 300 sensors per plant report at 10Hz (3000 events/sec/plant). Operators watch dashboards; far-zoom view doesn't need 10Hz, near-zoom does. Anomalies (temp spike, vibration) trigger immediate alerts. 50 plants. If wrong: missed equipment failures cost $1M+ per incident, regulatory non-compliance for missed alert SLA.
constraints: Ingest 3000 events/sec/plant durably
Time-series storage with efficient range queries
Server-side downsampling per viewer zoom level
Anomaly detection within 500ms of event
Alert push delivery guarantee — must reach all on-call engineers
failure_modes_to_handle: Sensor floods (broken sensor sends 10kHz)
Hypertable chunk boundary skew during ingest spike
Viewer requests 30-day range query → must not lock table
Alert delivery while all on-call sockets disconnected
architecture_reasoning:
1. Devices push via MQTT bridge → backend ingester batch-inserts (every 100ms or 500 rows) into TimescaleDB hypertable. Socket.IO is for OPERATOR fan-out, not device ingest (devices use MQTT for backpressure semantics).
2. Live fan-out: ingester after commit publishes batched events to Redis 'plant:{id}:tick'. Socket.IO worker subscribes, applies per-viewer downsampling (every Nth point based on viewer's zoom), emits to viewer.
3. Anomaly: ingester runs simple threshold check on each row; if triggered, INSERT into alerts table → notify alerts:{plantId} → BullMQ job for redundant delivery (socket + SMS + email).
4. Downsampling: server keeps per-viewer 'desiredHz' set on subscribe. On batch arrival, drops samples to match. Far-zoom viewer at 1Hz receives 1/10 the data of near-zoom at 10Hz. Saves egress + client CPU.
5. TimescaleDB chosen: native time-series compression, continuous aggregates, chunk pruning. Mongo time-series collection is decent but TimescaleDB's continuous aggregates auto-maintain rolled-up views — perfect for zoom queries.
6. Adapter: Redis. Plant rooms span nodes.
7. Recovery: ingester is idempotent via (device_id, ts) PK. Viewer reconnect: client knows lastTs, server queries WHERE ts > lastTs ORDER BY ts LIMIT 10000 from appropriate continuous aggregate based on zoom.

database_design.indexes: PK (device_id, metric, ts) for upsert dedup + per-device queries. (plant_id, ts DESC) for live tail per plant. Continuous aggregates have their own composite index.
database_design.queries: Live tail: SELECT ... FROM sensor_events WHERE plant_id=$1 AND ts > now()-interval '30 seconds' (covered by index, hot chunk). Zoom-out: SELECT bucket, avg FROM sensor_1m WHERE plant_id=$1 AND bucket BETWEEN $2 AND $3. Replay: same but from appropriate aggregate based on range width. Anomaly check inline in ingester before INSERT for low latency.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Batched INSERT in single tx (1 round trip per 500 rows). Conflict policy ON CONFLICT (device_id,metric,ts) DO NOTHING — sensor replay = idempotent. Alert insert is single statement, no tx. No need for advisory locks here — PK serialization is enough.

socket_io_design.namespaces: /telemetry for live data, /alerts separate (different SLA, separate backpressure handling).
socket_io_design.rooms: plant:{plantId} for telemetry. alerts:{plantId} for alerts.
socket_io_design.events: type Tick = { deviceId:string; metric:string; ts:number; value:number }; type Alert = { id:string; deviceId:string; metric:string; value:number; threshold:number; ts:number }; Events: 'subscribe' (C→S {plantId, hz, devices?}), 'tick' (S→C batched array), 'alert' (S→C), 'alert:ack' (C→S), 'replay' (C→S {plantId, t0, t1, hz} ack with downsampled array).
socket_io_design.middleware_order: jwt-auth → plant-access-check → subscription-cap (max 1 plant per socket — prevent fan-out abuse)
socket_io_design.adapter_choice: redis. Telemetry pub/sub channel is independent of adapter (we pre-filter+downsample per-viewer).

race_conditions_addressed: Duplicate ingest from MQTT retry: PK (device_id, metric, ts) + ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING ensures exactly-once persistence.
Anomaly fires while alert table is locked (vacuum etc): inline INSERT awaits; downstream Redis publish only after commit. Worst case: alert delayed seconds, never lost.
Viewer changes hz mid-stream: lastSentAtPerKey persists, so reducing hz means next tick may exceed new interval immediately — acceptable.
Sensor floods (broken sensor at 10kHz): ingester batch-flushes faster, but downstream throttle per viewer caps egress. Set per-device ingest rate-limit at MQTT bridge to drop excess at source.
scaling_considerations: 10x (50k events/sec/plant): TimescaleDB compression keeps disk in check; chunks need to be partitioned across nodes (Timescale multinode). Ingester needs work-stealing pool. 100x: rethink — move to ClickHouse for OLAP query side, keep TimescaleDB for hot write side; replicate to Kafka.
observability: Metrics: ingest_lag_ms (ts → INSERT commit), batch_size, alert_create_to_emit_ms, downsample_drop_rate per viewer. Logs: anomaly with full sensor context. Alerts: ingest_lag > 1s (DB slow), alert delivery failure (no SMS ack within 30s).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: for-each event io.emit('data', e). Breaks: (1) 3000 emits/sec/plant × 50 viewers = 150k emits/sec → event-loop death; (2) no batching → adapter pub/sub crushed; (3) no downsampling → 10Hz to a 1px wide chart wastes 99% of bytes; (4) anomaly check after broadcast → stale; (5) Postgres without time-series → 100M-row table without chunk pruning means a 30-day query scans everything. Killer bug: a single broken sensor at 10kHz takes down ingest for the whole plant because the unbounded buffer eats all RAM.

title: WebRTC signaling for 8-person rooms with SFU coordination and reconnect-safe state
business_context: Video meeting product, up to 8 participants per room. Socket.IO does signaling only (SDP exchange, ICE candidates, mute state, screen share); media flows via SFU (mediasoup). Reconnecting to ongoing call must restore state without re-handshaking everyone. Scale: 10k concurrent rooms. If broken: meeting drops mid-pitch, customer leaves.
constraints: Signaling: <100ms ICE candidate exchange
Reconnect within 30s restores call without media interruption
Mute/camera/screenshare state authoritative on server
Per-participant SDP transport state preserved on signal-server restart
Coordination with SFU for transport allocation
failure_modes_to_handle: Signal server restart drops all sockets but media keeps flowing
ICE candidate trickle stops mid-handshake
Two clients try to join with same userId (tabs)
SFU worker death — must reroute
Late-joiner missing earlier SDP offers
architecture_reasoning:
1. Signaling layer is stateless: all room state in Mongo (participants, SFU transport IDs, mute states). Socket.IO is a thin layer that authenticates, validates, persists, and broadcasts.
2. Client A joins → POST /rooms/{id}/join → returns SFU node assignment → opens socket → requests transport-create → server calls SFU API to allocate transport → stores transport_id in participant doc → returns connection params → client connects to SFU directly for media. SDP exchange (offer/answer) happens via socket events relayed through room.
3. Reconnect: client socket drops; participant doc keeps transport_id + lastSeenAt. New socket connects with reconnectToken; server finds participant by token, rejoins room, resends current room snapshot (participants + their states), client resumes media using stored SFU connection (media never dropped).
4. Mongo chosen: room state is document-shaped, frequent mutations to participant subdocs benefit from $set on positional operators. Change streams broadcast state changes (mute, screenshare) cross-node without manual pub/sub.
5. Adapter: Redis. Room may span nodes.
6. Bottleneck: SFU API calls on transport-create are 50-200ms each — keep them off the hot path; do them in transport-create handler async.
7. Rejected: WebSocket-only signaling without persistence — server restart = all calls drop. Rejected hosting media + signal on same node — coupling makes restart catastrophic.

database_design.indexes: Participant (roomId, leftAt) for active-participant list. (reconnectToken) unique for resume.
database_design.queries: Active participants: db.participants.find({roomId, leftAt: null}). Resume: db.participants.findOne({reconnectToken}). Update mute: db.participants.updateOne({_id}, {$set:{mic:false}}).
database_design.transaction_strategy: Room creation + first participant in tx (so an abandoned room doesn't exist without anyone). State updates (mute) are atomic single-doc, no tx. Recording state transitions use findOneAndUpdate with state check to prevent two start-record races.

socket_io_design.rooms: room:{roomId}.
socket_io_design.events: type Snapshot = { roomId:string; participants: Participant[] }; type SdpEvt = { from:string; to:string; sdp:any }; type IceEvt = { from:string; to:string; candidate:any }; Events: 'room:join' (C→S {roomId, reconnectToken?}, ack with snapshot+transports), 'sdp' (C→S relayed via 'sdp' to target socket), 'ice' (C→S relayed), 'state:update' (C→S {mic?,cam?,screenSharing?}), 'participant:joined' (S→C), 'participant:left' (S→C), 'participant:state' (S→C), 'transport:create' (C→S, ack with SFU params), 'recording:start' (C→S host-only).
socket_io_design.middleware_order: jwt-auth → room-permission-check → rate-limit (60 events/sec, signaling is bursty)
socket_io_design.adapter_choice: redis. Room state mutations also fan out via Mongo change stream to keep multi-node nodes in sync.

race_conditions_addressed: Two tabs with same userId: each gets unique participant _id and reconnectToken; appear as separate participants. UI can dedup by userId if desired.
Reconnect during ongoing call: grace timeout on disconnect (30s) means leftAt not set immediately; reconnect with token re-attaches before timer fires.
Two clients race recording:start: findOneAndUpdate with recordingState:'off' condition succeeds for only one; second sees null and returns 'already_recording'.
Late ICE candidate after target disconnected: forward attempt finds no matching socket and silently drops — ICE timeout on sender handles cleanly.
scaling_considerations: 10x (100k rooms): mongo participant write rate (state updates) becomes hot — batch state updates with 100ms debounce client-side. SFU node pool needs auto-scaling. 100x: signal server itself becomes CPU-bound on event loop from SDP relay — shard /rtc namespace by room hash across dedicated signal clusters.
observability: Metrics: join_to_first_media_ms, signal_relay_p99, reconnect_success_rate, sfu_transport_create_ms. Logs: every join/leave with participantId, reconnect events, recording state transitions. Alerts: reconnect_success_rate < 95%, sfu_create_ms p99 > 1s (SFU saturated).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: io.to(room).emit('sdp', sdp) for everyone. Breaks: (1) SDP is point-to-point, broadcasting wastes bandwidth and confuses peers; (2) no participant persistence = signal server restart = call dies; (3) mute state local-only = UI shows wrong icon to others; (4) no reconnect token = a phone briefly losing wifi drops user from meeting. Killer bug: at 8 participants, full-mesh SDP exchange is 56 sends per state change; ALL going through signal — at 1000 rooms doing this, signal server falls behind by 5s and ICE handshakes time out.

title: Multi-user form editing with field-level locks and reliable autosave
business_context: Insurance claim form, 200 fields, must be filled by claimant + adjuster simultaneously. Field-level locking prevents overwrite. Autosave every 2s — losing work after a 20-min session is unacceptable (high-value claims). Scale: 5k concurrent forms, 5 collaborators each. If wrong: claims data corrupted = legal liability + customer escalation.
constraints: Per-field lock with auto-release on disconnect
Autosave debounced to 2s server-side
Recovery from network loss without duplicate writes
Who-is-editing-what indicator updates within 200ms
Form version history for audit
failure_modes_to_handle: User holds field lock then closes laptop
Two users click same field simultaneously
Save fires mid-typing causing lost characters
Reconnect after 5min — what locks does user still hold?
architecture_reasoning:
1. Field lock = Postgres advisory lock per (formId, fieldId). Session-scoped lock auto-releases on connection drop — perfect for our 'user disconnect releases their locks' semantic.
2. Lock acquire: server connection runs SELECT pg_try_advisory_lock(hashtext(formId), hashtext(fieldId)). If acquired, broadcast 'field:locked'. Server stores active-locks per socket (so on disconnect we know what to release/announce).
3. Autosave: client emits 'field:save' on debounced change. Server validates lock-held-by-user, UPDATE form_data SET fields = jsonb_set(fields, '{field}', value), version = version + 1 WHERE form_id = $1. Returns new version. INSERT into form_history captures snapshot.
4. Postgres chosen: advisory locks are the killer feature here; trivial in Postgres, awkward to fake in Mongo. JSONB for fields makes per-field updates clean.
5. Adapter: Redis. Form room may span nodes — but the lock is per-pg-session, and the pg session is per-node. Cross-node coordination via Redis: maintain Redis SET of {formId:fieldId → nodeId+userId} so other nodes know lock exists before attempting pg_try_advisory_lock locally.
6. Reconnect: locks released on socket disconnect (pg connection released to pool). User re-acquires after reconnect by re-emitting field:focus events.
7. Rejected: Mongo with TTL key as lock — TTL is eventual, allows races. Rejected single-node form routing — popular forms hot-spot.

database_design.schema:
CREATE TABLE forms (
  id BIGSERIAL PK,
  template_id BIGINT NOT NULL,
  owner_id BIGINT NOT NULL,
  status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'draft',
  version INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  fields JSONB NOT NULL DEFAULT '{}',
  updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);

CREATE TABLE form_history (
  id BIGSERIAL PK,
  form_id BIGINT NOT NULL,
  version INT NOT NULL,
  field_path TEXT NOT NULL,
  prev_value JSONB,
  new_value JSONB,
  changed_by BIGINT NOT NULL,
  changed_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  UNIQUE (form_id, version)
);
CREATE INDEX ON form_history (form_id, version DESC);

CREATE TABLE form_collaborators (
  form_id BIGINT NOT NULL,
  user_id BIGINT NOT NULL,
  role TEXT NOT NULL,
  PRIMARY KEY (form_id, user_id)
);
database_design.indexes: forms PK. form_history (form_id, version DESC) for audit playback. form_collaborators PK.
database_design.queries: Update field: UPDATE forms SET fields = jsonb_set(fields, $1, $2), version = version + 1, updated_at = now() WHERE id = $3 RETURNING version. Snapshot history: INSERT into form_history (...). Lock acquire: SELECT pg_try_advisory_lock(hashtext($form), hashtext($field)). Release: SELECT pg_advisory_unlock(hashtext($form), hashtext($field)).
database_design.transaction_strategy: Save field is single tx: SELECT pg_try_advisory_xact_lock(formId) to serialize concurrent field saves to same form (cheap), UPDATE forms returning new version, INSERT into form_history with that version. Tx commits → version consistent. Long-held field-focus locks use session-scoped pg_advisory_lock (NOT _xact_lock) so they survive across multiple saves until explicit release or disconnect.

socket_io_design.rooms: form:{formId}.
socket_io_design.events: type FieldLockEvt = { formId:string; fieldId:string; userId:string; }; type FieldSaveEvt = { formId:string; fieldId:string; value:any; expectedVersion:number }; Events: 'form:open' (C→S ack with form + active locks), 'field:focus' (C→S, server attempts lock and broadcasts), 'field:blur' (C→S, releases lock), 'field:save' (C→S, ack with newVersion or conflict), 'field:locked'/'field:released' (S→C broadcast), 'field:updated' (S→C broadcast).

race_conditions_addressed: Two users click same field simultaneously: pg_try_advisory_lock + redis.hsetnx ensure only one wins. The pg lock and Redis lock together prevent split-brain (Redis lost = pg still serializes; pg lock got but Redis race = we release pg and report lockedBy).

User disconnects holding lock: pool client release returns the pg connection; pg automatically releases session locks on disconnect. Redis cleanup happens in disconnect handler; if that handler fails, the pg lock is already gone so any retry succeeds (Redis becomes stale but next focus will reconcile).

Save during reconnect (request in flight when socket drops): client tracks pending saves; on reconnect, re-focuses fields with pending and re-saves with current version (server returns version_mismatch → client merges).
Autosave conflict from server-side merged change: expectedVersion check fails → client receives current fields and version, applies merge strategy (keep local value, increment version, retry).
scaling_considerations: 10x (50k forms, 25k collaborators): pool sizing — each focused field consumes a pg connection; need 5 collaborators × 5 active fields × 50k = 1.25M connections, infeasible. Solution: cap concurrent focused fields per user to 1, treat blur as immediate (drop pg session). 100x: switch from advisory locks to a leases table with TTL (less perfect, but scales).
observability: Metrics: lock_acquire_p99, lock_hold_duration_ms, save_conflict_rate, autosave_lag_ms (last keystroke → committed). Logs: every field save with formId, fieldId, userId, oldVer, newVer for audit. Alerts: pg connection pool exhaustion (held connections > 90%), save_conflict_rate > 5%.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: socket.on('change', (f,v) => { state[f]=v; io.emit('change', {f,v}); }) + periodic save to DB. Breaks: (1) two simultaneous edits = last-write-wins lost characters; (2) crash = lost edits since last save (could be 30s+); (3) no lock = both users type in same field, watch each other's text get clobbered; (4) no version = stale save overwrites newer save. Killer bug: at 5 collaborators, a slow saver's stale state overwrites everyone else's recent edits when their debounce finally fires.

title: Long-running job pipeline with per-stage progress, cancellation, and dead-letter handling
business_context: Video transcoding pipeline: upload → probe → transcode (5 renditions) → thumbnail → publish. Users watch live progress per stage. Cancellation must propagate to mid-stage workers. Failed jobs go to DLQ with retry semantics. Scale: 1000 concurrent jobs, 5min average duration. If broken: users abandon uploads → revenue loss.
constraints: Per-stage progress emitted at 1Hz max
Cancel reaches worker within 2s
Dead-letter after 3 retries with backoff
Reconnect mid-job restores current progress
Multi-tab user sees same progress on all tabs
failure_modes_to_handle: Worker dies mid-transcode
Cancel arrives after job completes
User watches job from 2 tabs
Server restart loses in-memory progress state
Stage emit storm (1000 frame updates/sec from FFmpeg)
architecture_reasoning:
1. Jobs persisted in Postgres, queued in BullMQ. Worker emits progress to Redis (not DB — too noisy). Socket.IO subscribes to Redis 'job:{id}:progress'.
2. Cancel: API sets jobs.status='cancel_requested' AND publishes 'job:cancel:{id}'. Workers periodically check Redis cancel flag at safe checkpoints (between FFmpeg passes).
3. Progress throttling: worker writes Redis 'job:{id}:progress' max 1Hz via local debounce; Socket.IO subscriber pushes to clients.
4. Postgres chosen: jobs need ACID for state machine (queued → running → ...→ done). BullMQ uses Redis but Postgres holds the truth.
5. Adapter: Redis. job:{jobId} room for fan-out.
6. DLQ: BullMQ failed events → INSERT into dead_letters; admin UI can re-enqueue.
7. Multi-tab: same userId joins user:{userId}:jobs room AND job:{jobId} room; we broadcast to job room so all interested sockets receive.
8. Rejected: Postgres LISTEN for progress — write storm. Rejected in-memory progress only — restart loses state.

database_design.schema:
CREATE TABLE jobs (
  id BIGSERIAL PK,
  user_id BIGINT NOT NULL,
  type TEXT NOT NULL,
  status TEXT NOT NULL CHECK (status IN ('queued','running','succeeded','failed','cancel_requested','cancelled')),
  stages JSONB NOT NULL DEFAULT '[]', -- [{name, status, progress, startedAt, endedAt, error}]
  payload JSONB NOT NULL,
  attempt INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  enqueued_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  started_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
  ended_at TIMESTAMPTZ
);
CREATE INDEX ON jobs (user_id, enqueued_at DESC);
CREATE INDEX ON jobs (status) WHERE status IN ('queued','running','cancel_requested');

CREATE TABLE dead_letters (
  id BIGSERIAL PK,
  job_id BIGINT NOT NULL REFERENCES jobs(id),
  attempt INT NOT NULL,
  error TEXT NOT NULL,
  payload JSONB,
  failed_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);
database_design.indexes: jobs (user_id, enqueued_at DESC) for user history. Partial index on status for in-flight scans.
database_design.queries: Pickup: BullMQ handles via Redis (Postgres jobs table is mirror). Stage complete: UPDATE jobs SET stages = jsonb_set(stages, '{0}', $1::jsonb) WHERE id=$2. Cancel: UPDATE jobs SET status='cancel_requested' WHERE id=$1 AND status IN ('queued','running').
database_design.transaction_strategy: Stage transition tx: UPDATE jobs SET stages, status with row lock. DLQ insert + jobs.status='failed' in tx so dead_letters and final status are atomic.

socket_io_design.rooms: job:{jobId} for that job's subscribers. user:{userId}:jobs for user's overall list view.
socket_io_design.events: type StageProgress = { jobId:string; stage:string; progress:number; status:string }; Events: 'jobs:subscribe' (C→S, joins user room, ack with active jobs), 'job:subscribe' (C→S {jobId}, joins job room, ack with full state), 'job:cancel' (C→S, returns ack), 'job:progress' (S→C broadcast), 'job:stage' (S→C broadcast on stage transition), 'job:done' (S→C terminal).

race_conditions_addressed: Cancel arrives after job done: SQL UPDATE WHERE status IN ('queued','running') is a no-op; ack returns 'not_cancellable'.
Worker crash mid-stage: BullMQ retries; attempt counter increments; after 3, DLQ. Job state in jobs.stages JSONB reflects last persisted progress.
Reconnect mid-job: client re-emits job:subscribe; server returns full state + last cached progress from Redis. No event-replay needed — current state is enough.
Progress storm from FFmpeg (1000/sec): worker-side per-jobId rate-limit at 1Hz before Redis publish — Socket.IO bus never sees the storm.
Multi-tab user with same job: both tabs join job:{id} room; each ack-driven snapshot then subscribes to live updates; no duplicate events because Socket.IO room emit fans to all sockets in room.
scaling_considerations: 10x (10k concurrent): BullMQ Redis can handle; worker pool needs autoscaling per stage type. Postgres jobs table grows — partition by created_at month. 100x: stages JSONB becomes hot for updates; switch to job_stages table with per-stage rows.
observability: Metrics: stage_duration_ms histogram per stage, cancel_to_stop_ms (how fast workers honor cancel), retry_count, dlq_rate. Logs: stage transitions with jobId, attempt. Alerts: dlq_rate > 1%, p99 stage_duration > 10x median (worker stuck), cancel_to_stop_ms p99 > 5s.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: in-memory job map + io.emit('progress', pct) every event. Breaks: (1) server restart = lost progress, users see frozen progress bar; (2) FFmpeg emits 1000 progress events/sec → adapter saturated; (3) cancel never reaches worker; (4) retry/DLQ not in PG = lost failure context. Killer bug: at 200 concurrent jobs, the FFmpeg progress stream fan-out alone consumes 50% of Node CPU, slowing everything else.

title: Multi-cursor presence with viewport-following and selection sharing
business_context: Code editor product (VS Code style) wants Live Share: see coworker's cursor + selection + viewport in real-time. Optional 'follow user' mode auto-scrolls your editor to theirs. Scale: 10 collaborators per file, 50k active files. If clumsy: cursors flicker, follow stutters, users disable feature.
constraints: Cursor updates throttled to 30Hz on wire, 60Hz on client
Selection ranges (start/end) update on selection-change
Follow mode: smooth scrolling to leader's viewport at 60fps
