Reconnect restores other users' cursors without flicker
failure_modes_to_handle: Cursor flood from key-holding
Follow leader scrolls 1000 lines instantly
Leader closes file while followers attached
Multiple followers of same leader
architecture_reasoning:
1. Cursor + viewport is ephemeral — Redis hash per file. No persistence; on reconnect, fetch current snapshot.
2. Client emits 'cursor:move' throttled to 30Hz. Server stores in Redis + publishes to file room. Server can rate-limit emergency throttle if a client floods.
3. Follow: client emits 'follow:start' targeting leader. Server tags follower in Mongo collaborator_follow. Leader's viewport events get amplified to followers (server-side: re-emit specifically to followers as 'follow:viewport').
4. Mongo for collaborator metadata (who's in file, who follows whom). Redis for ephemeral cursors.
5. Adapter: Redis. File room.
6. Rejected: persisting cursors — useless on reconnect (old position stale).

database_design.indexes: Collaborator (fileId) for roster. lastSeenAt for stale cleanup.
database_design.queries: Roster: db.collaborators.find({fileId, lastSeenAt: {$gte: 60sAgo}}). Update lastSeen: updateOne({_id}, {$set:{lastSeenAt:now}}). Cursor: HSET/HGET on Redis hash.
database_design.transaction_strategy: None needed — single-doc updates and Redis writes.

socket_io_design.rooms: file:{fileId}.
socket_io_design.events: type Cursor = { userId:string; line:number; col:number; selStart?:{line:number;col:number}; selEnd?:{line:number;col:number}; viewportTop:number; viewportBottom:number; ts:number }; Events: 'file:join' (C→S ack with roster + cursors), 'cursor:move' (C→S throttled), 'cursor:change' (S→C broadcast), 'follow:start' (C→S {leaderId}), 'follow:stop' (C→S), 'follow:viewport' (S→C to follower).
socket_io_design.middleware_order: jwt-auth → file-read-check → rate-limit (40 cursor moves/sec/socket; flood = disconnect)

race_conditions_addressed: Cursor flood from key-holding: server-side hard rate-limit + flood counter disconnects abusive sockets; client also caps at 60Hz.
Follow leader scrolls 1000 lines: client receives single viewport event but applies smooth lerp animation (16ms per frame); user sees scrolling not teleport.
Leader closes file while followers attached: disconnect deletes collaborator + cursor; followers receive 'collab:left' and UI auto-stops follow.
Reconnect: cursor was deleted on disconnect; on rejoin, snapshot returns current cursors of others (own cursor not yet emitted by us — populated on first move).
scaling_considerations: 10x (100 collaborators/file): cursor emit rate × room size grows quadratically — degrade gracefully by reducing per-user broadcast rate as room grows. 100x: split into shards by fileId; viewport amplification cost = O(followers); cap followers per leader at 50.
observability: Metrics: cursor_emit_rate per socket, follow_count gauge, flood_disconnect_count. Logs: flood disconnects with userId. Alerts: flood_disconnect_rate spike (DDoS?).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: socket.on('cursor', c => io.to(file).emit('cursor', c)). Breaks: (1) no rate-limit = key-holding floods room; (2) no roster = colors flicker on reconnect (new color each time); (3) no follow amplification or, worse, follow as 'subscribe to leader's events' breaks when leader's tab closes mid-emit. Killer bug: at 10 collaborators all typing, 10 × 100Hz = 1000 cursor events/sec/file × N files = bus melts.

title: Threaded replies with @mentions firing notifications and search across history
business_context: Customer-support team chat needs threaded replies (Slack-style), @mentions that ping the mentioned person (even if not in thread), and full-text search across all messages including old threads. Scale: 1M messages, 500 active threads. If wrong: support tickets get lost in chat → SLA breaches.
constraints: Reply count + last reply time updates live on parent
@mention triggers notification even if recipient not subscribed
Full-text search across all history with relevance ranking
Edit/delete of parent message propagates to all open thread views
failure_modes_to_handle: Mention of user who is offline
Search returns 100k rows
Thread parent deleted while thread is open
Mention storm in viral message
architecture_reasoning:
1. Messages in Postgres with parent_id self-FK for threads. tsvector column for full-text search; trigger maintains it.
2. Mention detection: server parses @user tokens on send → INSERT mention records → enqueue notification (uses sock-008's notification pipeline). Mention notifications include 'jump to thread' deep link.
3. Live reply count: trigger on INSERT/DELETE/UPDATE of messages with parent_id maintains a denormalized counter on parent (replies_count, last_reply_at). NOTIFY fires on parent change so open thread views update.
4. Postgres tsvector + GIN index for search; ranked by ts_rank.
5. Adapter: Redis. thread:{parentMsgId} room for thread subscribers, channel:{id} for top-level (from sock-002).
6. Rejected: separate search service (Elasticsearch) — overkill until 10M+ messages.

database_design.indexes: GIN(tsv) for fulltext. (parent_id, id) for thread fetch. mentions (mentioned_user_id, notified) for backlog scan.
database_design.queries: Thread fetch: SELECT * FROM messages WHERE parent_id=$1 AND deleted_at IS NULL ORDER BY id ASC. Search: SELECT id, body, ts_rank(tsv, q) AS rank FROM messages, to_tsquery('english', $1) q WHERE tsv @@ q AND channel_id IN (...accessible...) ORDER BY rank DESC LIMIT 50.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Send-with-mentions: tx wrapping INSERT messages → for each parsed @ token, INSERT into mentions ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING → enqueue notification job. Single tx ensures no orphaned mentions if notification enqueue fails.

socket_io_design.rooms: thread:{parentMsgId} for thread subscribers. Channel room already exists.
socket_io_design.events: type Reply = Msg & { parentId:string }; Events: 'thread:open' (C→S ack with replies), 'thread:close', 'reply:new' (S→C in thread room), 'parent:updated' (S→C in channel room when reply count changes — for UI badge), 'search' (HTTP, not socket — too heavy for real-time).

race_conditions_addressed: Mention storm in viral message: notification grouping (sock-008) collapses N mentions of same user/target into one within 5min.
Thread parent deleted while open: 'msg:deleted' from channel broadcast tells thread view to close gracefully; replies remain in DB referencing deleted parent for forensic.
Edit of parent: tsvector trigger re-indexes; mentions parsed-on-edit are NOT inserted (mentions parsed only on initial send to avoid notification spam from edits).
Two replies arrive in different order across nodes: client sorts by id ASC on every state update, so insert order doesn't matter.
scaling_considerations: 10x (10M messages): GIN index for tsvector grows; consider per-channel partitioning. 100x: move search to Elasticsearch with CDC from Postgres logical replication; keep Postgres for transactional reads.
observability: Metrics: search_query_ms, mention_extract_count_per_msg, parent_update_emit_latency. Logs: every mention with mentioner/mentionee. Alerts: search_query p99 > 2s.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: parse @ in client, fire separate /notify call per mention. Breaks: (1) client trust = users forge mentions of CEO; (2) no DB record = retry duplicates notifications; (3) no tsvector = LIKE '%foo%' table scan, dies at 100k messages. Killer bug: at 1k channels with active threads, every reply UPDATEs parent triggering NOTIFY which all nodes process even if no one cares about that channel — bus saturated.

title: Multi-region status board with regional aggregation and alert push
business_context: DevOps wall-mounted status board: 200 services × 12 regions = 2400 health tiles. Aggregations roll up by service and by region. Critical alerts (5xx spike, latency p99 > SLA) flash + audible. Scale: 30 viewers per board, change rate 100 status changes/min. If wrong: ops team misses outage onset → MTTR spikes.
constraints: Status update propagated in <1s
Tile-level subscribe (board only renders visible tiles)
Alert flash must not be missed (require ack click)
Server restart restores last-known state immediately
failure_modes_to_handle: Status flap (red→green→red within seconds)
Region partition reports all-red but is actually up
Viewer scrolls fast: subscribe/unsubscribe flood
Change stream drops with no resume token
architecture_reasoning:
1. Mongo holds current status per (service, region) document. Change stream pipeline filters operationType=update. Worker republishes via Redis pub/sub channel per service.
2. Flap suppression: server tracks transitions per tile; if >3 changes in 30s, escalate to 'flapping' status not raw color. Avoids alert fatigue.
3. Viewer subscribes per tile (service+region) via 'tile:watch' batched. Server tracks subs in memory.
4. Alerts: separate collection, append-only. Server emits alert on insert via change stream; client requires ack before clearing.
5. Mongo chosen for change streams and natural document model (status doc per tile). Postgres LISTEN/NOTIFY would work too but the rollup logic is easier with Mongo $facet aggregation.
6. Adapter: Redis. tile:{service}:{region} rooms.
7. Recovery: client tracks lastSeenAt per tile; on reconnect, GET /tiles?since=lastSeen gives recent transitions.

database_design.indexes: Status by service or region for rollup queries. Alerts (resolvedAt, createdAt) for active alerts list.
database_design.queries: Tile snapshot: db.statuses.findOne({_id}). Rollup by service: db.statuses.aggregate([{$match:{service:s}},{$group:{_id:'$status',n:{$sum:1}}}]). Active alerts: db.alerts.find({resolvedAt:null}).sort({createdAt:-1}).
database_design.transaction_strategy: None needed — single-doc updates. Status transitions could use findOneAndUpdate with transitionsLast30s increment + conditional flapping flag set.

socket_io_design.rooms: tile:{svc}:{region} per tile. board:{boardId} for whole-board events (alerts, flapping).
socket_io_design.events: type Tile = { service:string; region:string; status:string; p99:number; errorRate:number; lastChangedAt:string }; Events: 'board:open' (C→S returns initial 200x12 grid), 'tile:watch'/'tile:unwatch' (C→S batched), 'tile:update' (S→C), 'alert:new' (S→C), 'alert:ack' (C→S).

race_conditions_addressed: Status flap: transitionsLast30s counter + flapping label suppresses alert thrash.
Alert ack from two ops: $addToSet idempotent, both names captured.
Region partition reporting all-red: alert ingest from agent should require agreement (not implemented here but documented as upstream concern).
Subscribe/unsubscribe flood from fast scroll: socket.io room ops are cheap, but flood = network — debounce on client side (200ms).
scaling_considerations: 10x (24k tiles): single change-stream cursor handles; client memory at 24k tiles is fine. 100x (240k tiles): partition status collection by service hash; multiple change-stream workers.
observability: Metrics: status_change_to_emit_ms, flap_label_rate, unacked_critical_alert_count gauge. Logs: every status transition, every flap label. Alerts: unacked_critical > 0 for 5min (ops not watching!).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: poll /status every 1s, broadcast full board on any change. Breaks: (1) 2400 cells × 30 viewers × 1Hz = 72k tile renders/sec wasted; (2) no flap suppression = alert deluge; (3) ack global not per-user = anyone clearing it hides it for all. Killer bug: at fast scroll on a 2400-tile grid with no virtualization, browser dies.

title: Lobby and matchmaking with skill-based queuing and party support
business_context: Game lobby: solo + party (2-5 players) queue; matchmaker pairs by MMR within 200 points, expanding window over time. Match assignment pushed via socket. Party leader controls queue. Scale: 30k concurrent queuers, 1k matches/min throughput. If wrong: long queues, bad matches, players leave.
constraints: Match within 30s for popular skill bands
Party stays together always
Cancel queue cleanly without ghost match
Multi-region routing — match assigned to closest data center
failure_modes_to_handle: Player disconnects after queue but before match accept
Party leader dropped during queue
MMR pool empty — must expand window
Two matchmakers pick same player
architecture_reasoning:
1. Queue is Redis sorted set per region:mode:mmrBand → score=joinedAt. Matchmaker worker periodically pops batches and forms matches.
2. Party state in Mongo (durable across disconnects). Leader has authority to queue/cancel.
3. Match assignment: matchmaker INSERTs match doc with player list → broadcasts to each player's user:{id}:lobby room with 'match:found'. Players must accept within 15s; non-accepts return others to queue.
4. Mongo for parties + match docs (transactional party membership). Redis for queue (TTL, sorted set ops).
5. Adapter: Redis. Multi-node.
6. Race against double-pick: SETNX on player:lock:{userId} with TTL 30s when matchmaker grabs a player.

database_design.indexes: Party.memberIds for 'my party' lookup. Party (status, queuedAt) for matchmaker scan if Redis lost. TTL on MatchAssignment.
database_design.queries: Get my party: db.parties.findOne({memberIds: userId}). Leader-only queue: updateOne({_id, leaderId: userId}, {$set:{status:'queued', queuedAt:now}}). Match insert: insertOne with players.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Party join: tx adds member, validates capacity. Match accept: findOneAndUpdate to set acceptances.{userId}=true; when count == playerIds.length, set status='accepted' and trigger game server provisioning.

socket_io_design.rooms: user:{id}:lobby (multi-device), party:{partyId}.
socket_io_design.events: Events: 'party:create', 'party:invite', 'party:join', 'party:leave', 'queue:start' (leader only), 'queue:cancel', 'match:found' (S→C with acceptUntil), 'match:accept', 'match:decline'.

race_conditions_addressed: Two matchmakers pick same party: SETNX lock per player. Loser releases its own locks.
Player disconnects mid-queue: party stays queued. On match:found, if user offline, they miss the accept window → match:decline cleanup + others requeued.
Leader changes party mid-queue: queue:cancel → re-queue with new state.
Match accepted by all but provision fails: TTL on MatchAssignment cleans up; players notified via match:cancelled (extension).
scaling_considerations: 10x (300k queuers): matchmaker is single-threaded per region:mode; partition further by mmrBand. 100x: matchmaker becomes distributed; use Redis Streams for queue with consumer groups.
observability: Metrics: queue_wait_time_p50/p99, match_acceptance_rate, double_pick_attempts, matchmaker_tick_duration_ms. Logs: every assignment + acceptances. Alerts: queue_wait > 5min for popular mode (matchmaker stuck).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: io.to('queue').emit when 10 join. Breaks: (1) no MMR; (2) no party preservation; (3) double-pick races; (4) cancel races. Killer bug: at 1000 concurrent queue:start in 1s, all matchmaker instances grab same head-of-queue parties → most fail → queue stalls.

title: Order book streaming for crypto exchange with delta updates and snapshot bootstrap
business_context: Crypto exchange order book: 100 levels deep × bids/asks. Updates at 10Hz peak. Client must bootstrap from snapshot then apply deltas in order without gaps. Scale: 50 trading pairs × 5k viewers each. If wrong: bad UI = traders see stale book = bad trades = lawsuits.
constraints: Snapshot+delta protocol with sequence numbers
Gap detection triggers automatic resnapshot
Per-symbol fan-out without cross-symbol contention
Bandwidth: delta payload < 1KB typical
failure_modes_to_handle: Client misses delta sequence
Symbol-specific feed dies
Burst of 1000 deltas/sec on big trade
Reconnect during delta stream
architecture_reasoning:
1. Order book state lives in Redis (sorted sets per side per symbol). Trading engine writes deltas, increments per-symbol seq, publishes Redis pub/sub channel.
2. Snapshots: every 100 deltas (or 10s), publish full snapshot to a 'snapshot:{sym}' Redis hash + seq.
3. Postgres for trades (immutable historical record) — joinable for trade history.
4. Client bootstrap: HTTP GET /book/{sym}/snapshot → snapshot+lastSeq → connect socket → subscribe → deltas with seq. If client sees gap (gotSeq != expected), force resnapshot.
5. Adapter: Redis. symbol:{sym} room.

database_design.indexes: trades (symbol, ts DESC) for tape view.
database_design.queries: Snapshot via Redis: ZRANGE book:bids:{sym} 0 99 WITHSCORES + asks. Recent trades: SELECT FROM trades WHERE symbol=$1 ORDER BY id DESC LIMIT 100.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Trade engine handles atomicity; Postgres INSERT is single statement.

socket_io_design.rooms: sym:{symbol} per symbol.
socket_io_design.events: type Snapshot = { sym:string; seq:number; bids:[price,size][]; asks:[price,size][] }; type Delta = { sym:string; seq:number; side:'b'|'a'; price:number; size:number }; Events: 'sym:subscribe', 'sym:unsubscribe', 'snapshot' (S→C), 'delta' (S→C), 'resnapshot' (C→S request).

race_conditions_addressed: Delta gap on reconnect: client compares seq, requests resnapshot.
Multiple snapshot/delta arrival interleaving: seq is monotonic per symbol; deltas applied only if seq+1.
Stale delta after resnap: snapshot seq overrides; deltas < snapshot seq are ignored implicitly because they'd fail seq+1 check.
scaling_considerations: 10x (50k viewers/symbol): per-symbol fan-out is the limit; shard Redis adapter by symbol hash. 100x: introduce edge fan-out (push deltas to per-region edge servers, clients subscribe to nearest).
observability: Metrics: delta_emit_to_render_ms, gap_resnap_rate, snapshot_size_bytes. Logs: every resnap with sym + gap size. Alerts: gap_resnap_rate > 1% (feed unstable).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: emit full book on every change. Breaks: (1) bandwidth 100×; (2) no gap detection; (3) snapshot inside Socket.IO event = bandwidth burst on every reconnect. Killer bug: 1000 deltas/sec × 5k viewers × 50 symbols emits-full-book = 250GB/s. Server explodes.

title: 'Active in document' fine-grained presence with privacy scoping
business_context: Doc-collab product: show 'Alice is in Q3 Report', not just 'Alice is online'. Workspace admin sets visibility (everyone vs. team only vs. doc-collaborators only). Scale: 100k users, 5M docs. If wrong: privacy leak — confidential doc title exposed via presence.
constraints: Granular: per-doc presence
Privacy: viewer only sees docs they can access
Scope changes apply immediately to live sessions
Cleanup on tab close + grace for refresh
failure_modes_to_handle: User opens 20 doc tabs (which is 'active'?)
Permission revoked while user is viewing doc
Network blip — should not flap presence
Race: presence change vs. permission change
architecture_reasoning:
1. Active-doc tracked per (userId, docId) in Mongo with lastSeenAt + tabId. Active = lastSeenAt within 30s.
2. Privacy: when emitting 'X is in doc Y', server checks viewer can see Y. Filter at emit time.
3. Mongo chosen — joins to doc.permissions natural here; we already store docs in Mongo (sock-001).
4. Adapter: Redis. workspace:{wsId}:presence room.

database_design.indexes: By workspaceId+docId for 'who's in this doc'. TTL on lastSeenAt 60s for auto-cleanup.
database_design.queries: Who's in doc: db.activeDocs.find({docId, lastSeenAt:{$gte: now-30s}}). Where is user: db.activeDocs.find({userId, lastSeenAt:{$gte: now-30s}}).
database_design.transaction_strategy: None — single-doc upserts.

socket_io_design.rooms: ws:{workspaceId} for fan-out. doc:{docId}:presence for fine-grained subscribers.
socket_io_design.events: Events: 'doc:active' (C→S {docId,tabId}), 'doc:inactive', 'doc:hb' (C→S 20s), 'doc:presence' (S→C with permission filter).

race_conditions_addressed: Multi-tab: each tab has unique tabId; closing one tab doesn't claim user left doc.
Permission revoked: fan-out filters at emit time; viewers stop seeing presence.
Network blip: TTL 60s + hb 20s allows 2 missed beats; no spurious 'left'.
scaling_considerations: 10x: presence collection write rate dominates — batch heartbeats per socket (one hb covers all open docs).
observability: Metrics: doc_presence_emit_rate, hb_lag.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: broadcast 'X is in Y' to all. Breaks: confidential title leaks via presence even if user can't open the doc.

title: Annotation reactions and reply threads with optimistic counters
business_context: Annotation thread can have replies + emoji reactions (👍, ❤️, etc.). Reaction counts update live; threads grow live. Reactions optimistic on click but reconcile to server count. Scale: 1k reactions/sec on viral annotation.
constraints: Reaction toggle (add/remove) idempotent per user
Counter accurate under 1k/sec contention
Reply notifications to annotation author
Live count updates without per-emit DB query
failure_modes_to_handle: Double-click spam
Two devices same user reacting
Counter drift
Reply while annotation deleted
architecture_reasoning:
1. Reactions in Mongo: subdoc {emoji: [userIds]}. Toggle uses $addToSet/$pull conditionally.
2. Counter: maintained denormalized on annotation document via $size projection or separate counter; for hot annotations use Redis HINCRBY cache + reconciliation.
3. Replies in Mongo as separate collection (mirrors sock-007). Notification to annotation author via sock-008 pipeline.
4. Hybrid: annotations + replies in Mongo, hot counter cache in Redis (HASH reactions:{annoId} → emoji:count). Postgres unnecessary here.
5. Adapter: Redis.

database_design.indexes: Reply (annotationId, createdAt).
database_design.queries: Toggle reaction: findOneAndUpdate with conditional addToSet/pull on reactions.userIds. Bulk counts: from Redis HGETALL.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Single doc update is atomic; no tx needed.

socket_io_design.rooms: anno:{id} for thread subscribers, doc:{id} for counter updates.
socket_io_design.events: Events: 'anno:thread:open' (C→S ack with replies + reaction state), 'anno:react' (C→S {annoId, emoji, add}), 'anno:reply' (C→S), 'anno:reaction:update' (S→C with counts), 'anno:reply:new' (S→C).

race_conditions_addressed: Double-click spam: $addToSet idempotent — second click no-op. UI toggle uses local state for instant feedback.
Two devices same user: $addToSet ensures userId appears once.
Reply on deleted annotation: server check rejects.
Counter drift: server returns counts in ack, client reconciles.
scaling_considerations: 10x: hot annotation reactions become a write hotspot — switch to per-(anno,emoji,userId) collection with periodic aggregation.
observability: Metrics: reaction_toggle_p99, count_drift_event_count.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: store count integer, $inc on react. Breaks: same user can pump counter via spam since no userId record.

title: Cross-channel notification with do-not-disturb hours and per-channel preferences
business_context: Enterprise app: notifications via in-app, email, SMS, push, Slack. User configures: 'urgent → SMS, regular → in-app only, work hours only'. DND window suppresses non-urgent. Scale: 5M users, 1M notifications/min during business hours. If wrong: angry users muting app entirely.
constraints: Honor per-user channel preferences
DND window per timezone
Urgent overrides DND
Channel-specific dedup (in-app collapsing doesn't dedupe SMS)
Audit: which channel was actually used
failure_modes_to_handle: User timezone change mid-DND
Channel provider outage (Slack down)
Urgency promoted after initial dispatch
User unsubscribes mid-batch
architecture_reasoning:
1. Notification create → POST notifications (Postgres, durable, audit-friendly) → enqueue 'route' job → router decides channels based on user prefs + urgency + DND → enqueues per-channel jobs.
2. User prefs in Mongo (flexible schema: per-org rules, per-team overrides, frequency caps).
3. Channel workers (BullMQ): in-app, email, sms, push, slack. Each handles its own retries + DLQ.
4. DND check: simple time-window query against user.timezone (stored in prefs). Urgent bypasses.
5. Adapter: Redis for in-app channel only (sockets).
6. Hybrid: PG for notification audit (transactional), Mongo for prefs (frequently-edited document).

database_design.indexes: notifications (user_id, created_at). dispatches UNIQUE for dedup. Prefs is keyed by userId.
database_design.queries: Router decision per channel: SELECT prefs + dnd check. Audit list: SELECT FROM dispatches WHERE notification_id=$1.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Router enqueues per-channel jobs in tx with insert into dispatches (status=enqueued); avoids enqueue-without-record.

socket_io_design.rooms: user:{userId}.
socket_io_design.events: As sock-008 plus 'prefs:update' (C→S) and 'prefs:current' (S→C).

race_conditions_addressed: Prefs updated mid-route: router fetches prefs once per notification at start; outdated routing accepted as 'best effort current'.
Channel down: per-channel BullMQ retries with backoff; eventually DLQ if persistent.
Urgent re-evaluation: caller can update notifications.urgency before dispatch; router job re-checks at run time.
Cap exceeded: returned to caller as channel skipped; alternative channels still dispatched.
scaling_considerations: 10x (10M users): router decision becomes CPU hot — cache prefs in Redis 60s TTL. 100x: shard channel workers by userId hash.
observability: Metrics: dispatch_count by channel + status, dnd_skip_rate, cap_skip_rate, channel_provider_latency. Logs: every dispatch decision. Alerts: SMS dispatch failure > 5%, in-app delivered drops to 0 (socket layer broken).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: for each notification, send via all channels. Breaks: (1) ignores user prefs; (2) DND ignored; (3) cap ignored = unsubscribe spike; (4) no audit. Killer bug: SMS cost runaway when one user generates 1k notifications/hour, $100s in Twilio fees per hour.

title: Wearable health telemetry with anomaly ML model and per-patient alert routing
business_context: Remote patient monitoring: 50k patients × 4 sensors at 1Hz (HR, SpO2, ECG, BP). Edge-trained model flags arrhythmia in real-time, alerts on-call clinician. Patient data is PHI. If wrong: missed cardiac events = patient harm + HIPAA fines.
constraints: Per-patient isolation (no cross-patient leak)
Anomaly detection within 5s of sample
On-call clinician routing follows shift schedule
HIPAA audit log for every read
Ingest at 200k samples/sec aggregate
failure_modes_to_handle: Wearable disconnect = gap in data
Multiple clinicians viewing same patient
Shift change mid-alert
Model false-positive flood
architecture_reasoning:
1. Edge devices push via MQTT/HTTPS → ingester normalizes → Mongo time-series collection (per-metric). Inline anomaly scoring against on-device-distilled model.
2. Alert routing: shift schedule in Mongo. On anomaly, query active clinician for patient's team; emit to that clinician + supervisor.
3. Mongo time-series for HIPAA: per-patient indexes, field-level encryption for sensitive (BP, ECG raw).
4. Adapter: Redis. patient:{id} room.
5. Audit: every server-side read INSERTs audit_log row.

database_design.indexes: Samples: implicit per time-series + metaField. Anomalies: (patientId, ts). Shift: (teamId, startsAt, endsAt) for active-clinician lookup.
database_design.queries: Active clinician: db.shifts.findOne({teamId, startsAt:{$lte:now}, endsAt:{$gt:now}}). Recent samples: db.samples.find({patientId, metric, ts:{$gte:t0}}).sort({ts:-1}).
database_design.transaction_strategy: Audit + read in tx is unnecessary (audit can be eventually consistent — async write).

socket_io_design.rooms: patient:{id} per patient.
socket_io_design.events: Events: 'patient:open' (C→S ack with recent samples), 'patient:close', 'sample' (S→C), 'anomaly' (S→C), 'anomaly:ack' (C→S), 'patient:hb' (S→C if device offline).

race_conditions_addressed: Shift change mid-alert: shift query at alert-fire time picks current clinician. If shift just ended, the recently-off clinician misses; supervisor cc'd.
Multiple clinicians: room broadcast to all; anomaly:ack records who acked.
Wearable disconnect: ingester-side heartbeat absence triggers offline event; UI shows gap.
Anomaly storm (broken sensor): model can be wrong; soft-suppress similar anomalies within 60s window.
scaling_considerations: 10x: Mongo time-series scales via compression; ingester needs shards. 100x: separate read replicas for clinician views.
observability: Metrics: sample_ingest_lag, anomaly_to_alert_ms, ack_latency. Logs: every view (HIPAA audit). Alerts: anomaly_to_alert > 5s, ingest_lag > 1s.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: broadcast to all clinicians. Breaks privacy (clinician B sees patient assigned to A); shift unaware; no audit. HIPAA fines.

title: Screen share with viewer-only role and host moderation controls
business_context: Webinar with one host + 500 viewers (no media upstream). Host can promote viewer to speaker; viewer can request to speak. Mute-all, kick, end-event. Scale: 50 concurrent webinars. If broken: webinar embarrassment, refunds.
constraints: Viewer connection isn't full WebRTC peer — only consumer of SFU output
Host can mute/kick instantly
Viewer hand-raise → host promotes → adds upstream transport
Recording per-event
Late joiner sees current room state
failure_modes_to_handle: Host disconnect mid-event
Viewer floods 'hand:raise' events
Promoted viewer's mic is silent
Recording cleanup on event end
architecture_reasoning: 1. PG for event lifecycle (audit + ticketing joins). Reuse sock-010 SFU pattern for host's producer; viewers are consumer-only.\n2. Role state in PG event_roles with PK constraints to prevent dual roles.\n3. Adapter: Redis.

database_design.schema: CREATE TABLE events (id BIGSERIAL PK, host_user_id BIGINT, sfu_node_id TEXT, status TEXT, recording_url TEXT, started_at TIMESTAMPTZ, ended_at TIMESTAMPTZ);\nCREATE TABLE event_participants (event_id BIGINT, user_id BIGINT, role TEXT CHECK (role IN ('host','co-host','speaker','viewer')), joined_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now(), left_at TIMESTAMPTZ, hand_raised_at TIMESTAMPTZ, PRIMARY KEY (event_id, user_id));\nCREATE TABLE event_actions (id BIGSERIAL PK, event_id BIGINT, actor_id BIGINT, target_id BIGINT, action TEXT, at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now());
database_design.indexes: event_participants PK. event_actions (event_id, at).
database_design.queries: Promote: UPDATE event_participants SET role='speaker' WHERE event_id=$1 AND user_id=$2 AND role='viewer'. Kick: UPDATE left_at=now.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Mute-all is bulk UPDATE in tx with audit row.

socket_io_design.rooms: event:{id}, event:{id}:hosts.
socket_io_design.events: Events: 'event:join', 'hand:raise', 'hand:lower', 'promote' (host), 'demote', 'mute:user', 'mute:all', 'kick', 'event:state' (S→C), 'speaker:list' (S→C), 'recording:start'.
socket_io_design.middleware_order: auth → role-check (host actions require host role)

race_conditions_addressed: Two hosts promote different viewers to single speaker slot: SQL UPDATE WHERE role='viewer' makes second update no-op.
Hand-raise spam: rate-limit middleware caps; PG WHERE hand_raised_at IS NULL idempotent.
Kick during their event:join: disconnect after UPDATE — they receive disconnect event and UI returns to lobby.
Host disconnect: co-host inherits via shift logic (or event auto-ends after 30s grace).
scaling_considerations: 10x (5000 viewers/event): viewer fan-out is just role broadcast (cheap). SFU bandwidth is the real limit — autoscale SFU.
observability: Metrics: hand_raise_rate, promotion_count, kick_count, event_duration, recording_size_mb. Logs: every host action (audit). Alerts: kick spike (host abuse?).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: role in JS map. Breaks: server restart loses roles, host's mute-all evaporates. No audit.

title: Wizard-style multi-step form with cross-step validation and co-authoring presence
business_context: Insurance application wizard: 12 steps, branching by answers. Multiple agents help one applicant fill in real-time. Step lock prevents same step being edited by two. Cross-step validation runs after each step (e.g., income vs claim amount). Scale: 1k sessions × 3 collaborators avg. If wrong: invalid applications submitted → rework.
constraints: Step-level lock with auto-release
Cross-step validation re-runs on relevant changes
Branch determination is server-side authoritative
Saved partial restores exactly on reconnect
failure_modes_to_handle: Branch change invalidates already-filled future steps
Two agents start same step simultaneously
Validation rule update mid-session
Reconnect mid-step submission
architecture_reasoning:
1. Mongo for application state — document shape varies per branch; embedded answers per step.
2. Step lock via Redis SETNX (simpler than per-field advisory locks since step-level is coarser).
3. Validation engine runs server-side after every submit; emits errors to all collaborators.
4. Adapter: Redis. app:{id} room.

database_design.indexes: _id PK. collaboratorIds for 'my apps' query.
database_design.queries: Update step: findOneAndUpdate with version increment + steps.{id} set. Branch recalc: server function on submit.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Step submit single-doc atomic. No tx needed.

socket_io_design.rooms: app:{appId}.
socket_io_design.events: Events: 'app:open', 'step:lock', 'step:unlock', 'step:submit', 'step:locked' (S→C broadcast), 'step:updated' (S→C), 'validation:errors' (S→C).

race_conditions_addressed: Two agents lock same step: SETNX wins for one.
Version conflict on submit: client refetches and retries.
Branch change invalidates future: client UI re-renders with new branch's steps; orphan data preserved in steps map for audit.
Lock held by disconnected user: keys have TTL 600s; client UI can show 'stale, take over' button after timeout.
scaling_considerations: 10x: app document grows — split steps to subcollection if size > 16MB.
observability: Metrics: step_lock_p99, validation_run_ms, branch_change_rate.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: independent step inputs without locking. Two agents fill same step = last write wins, data lost.

title: Order tracking from placement to delivery with multi-stage carrier events
business_context: E-commerce order: placed → paid → packed → shipped → in-transit → delivered. Carrier webhooks update status; customer watches live timeline. Multi-tab support. Scale: 500k active orders, 100 carrier events/sec. If wrong: support tickets ('where's my order?').
constraints: Carrier webhook idempotency
Out-of-order webhook handling (delivered arrives before in-transit)
Customer can subscribe to order from app or email link
Driver location stream (optional) at 1Hz
Multi-tab read consistency
failure_modes_to_handle: Duplicate webhook from carrier
Webhook for unknown order id
Status regression (delivered → in-transit)
Driver GPS gap
architecture_reasoning:
1. PG for orders (transactional, joinable to users, invoices). Mongo for driver location history (time-series).
2. Webhook handler: idempotency via (carrier, carrier_event_id) UNIQUE. Status state machine prevents regression.
3. Adapter: Redis. order:{id} room.
4. Driver location: Mongo time-series; emit at 1Hz max via per-driver throttle.

database_design.indexes: order_events UNIQUE for webhook dedup. (order_id, ts) for timeline. DriverLoc time-series.
database_design.queries: Append event: INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING. Timeline: SELECT FROM order_events WHERE order_id ORDER BY ts. Status update: UPDATE orders SET status WHERE id AND ordering allowed (CASE for state machine).
database_design.transaction_strategy: Webhook handler tx: INSERT event idempotent + UPDATE orders.status with state-machine guard (CASE WHEN status IN ('placed','paid') THEN $new ELSE status END).

socket_io_design.rooms: order:{id}, driver:{id} for location subscribers.
socket_io_design.events: Events: 'order:subscribe' (C→S ack with order + timeline), 'order:event' (S→C new event), 'driver:location' (S→C 1Hz throttled).
socket_io_design.middleware_order: auth → order-ownership (or signed-link token for email recipients)

race_conditions_addressed: Duplicate webhook: UNIQUE (carrier, carrier_event_id) makes ON CONFLICT idempotent.
Out-of-order webhook (delivered before in-transit): state machine WHERE newRank > curRank prevents regression; event still recorded in order_events for full timeline.
Unknown order tracking ID: 404; nothing inserted.
Driver GPS gap: ingester gap; client just shows last-known with stale indicator.
scaling_considerations: 10x: order_events partition by month. Webhook ingest pool sizing.
observability: Metrics: webhook_dedup_rate, webhook_to_emit_ms, state_regression_attempt_count. Logs: every webhook. Alerts: regression > 0 means upstream bug.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: UPDATE orders.status on every webhook. Breaks: out-of-order webhooks regress status; duplicates rebroadcast same event.

title: Capstone: multi-tenant SaaS analytics with per-tenant change streams and quota enforcement
business_context: Analytics SaaS — each tenant has their own dashboards reading their own events. Per-tenant quota: max 100 viewers, max 100 events/sec emit, max 1M events stored. Tenant isolation MUST be airtight (event-leak between tenants = breach). Scale: 5k tenants, peak 50k concurrent viewers, 50k events/sec aggregate.
constraints: Strict tenant isolation in every layer
Per-tenant quota with graceful degradation
Per-tenant change stream resume
Multi-region tenant routing
Audit log per cross-tenant boundary attempt
failure_modes_to_handle: Tenant A query accidentally returns tenant B rows
Single tenant DOS (one tenant consumes all resources)
Change stream cursor failure isolated per tenant
Tenant deletion mid-stream
architecture_reasoning:
1. PG (per-tenant schema) for transactional + auth; Mongo (per-tenant collection prefix) for events (change streams).
2. Tenant context in JWT; every query enforces tenant scope via middleware.
3. Quota: Redis token buckets per (tenant, resource).
4. Adapter: Redis. tenant:{id}:dash:{id} rooms namespaced.
5. Change streams: one cursor per tenant (resource cost) — only spin up if tenant has active subs; tear down on idle 5min.
6. Tenant deletion: drain sockets, close cursors, snapshot final data to S3, drop collections.

database_design.indexes: tenants PK. users (tenant_id, email). Mongo per-tenant collection has its own indexes including (createdAt) for change stream filter.
database_design.queries: All queries set app.tenant_id session var via pg pool before querying.
database_design.transaction_strategy: Standard tx wrapping multi-statement ops. RLS guards even in tx.

socket_io_design.rooms: tenant:{tid}:dash:{did}.
socket_io_design.events: Events: 'dash:subscribe', 'tick' (S→C), 'quota:warning', 'quota:exceeded'.
socket_io_design.adapter_choice: redis with key prefix per tenant for isolation: createAdapter(pub, sub, { key: `t${tenantId}` })? No — actual implementation uses single adapter but namespaces rooms with tenant prefix; cross-tenant emit attempts logged + rejected.

race_conditions_addressed: Cross-tenant subscribe attempt: tenant prefix in room + dashboard ownership check + audit log; even if a bug allows wrong room join, change-stream filter on per-tenant collection ensures no data leaks.
Quota race (many parallel posts hit cap at same second): INCR + EXPIRE atomic; first to exceed cap gets 429.
Cursor failure for one tenant: try/catch deletes from map; next subscribe re-creates from resume token. Other tenants unaffected.
Tenant deletion mid-stream: deletion worker iterates active sockets and disconnects, closes cursor, drops collection. Sockets reconnecting hit 'tenant_not_found' middleware error.
Viewer cap race (101 connect simultaneously): SCARD-then-SADD has TOCTOU; mitigate by using SADD-then-SCARD pattern and remove on overflow. Alternative: Lua atomic script.
scaling_considerations: 10x (50k tenants): tenant cursor count becomes the bottleneck — pool cursors with consistent hashing across N worker nodes so each tenant's cursor lives on exactly one node, others proxy via Redis pub/sub. 100x (500k tenants): per-tenant collection model breaks Mongo's collection-count limits — switch to single events collection partitioned by tenantId with $match in change stream.
observability: Metrics: per-tenant viewer_count, eps_in, eps_out, cursor_count gauge, cross_tenant_attempt_count, dashboard_subscribe_p99. Logs: every cross-tenant attempt, every quota breach. Traces: tenant_id in every span. Alerts: cross_tenant_attempt > 0 (security incident), tenant cursor failure rate > 1%, viewer cap reached for high-plan tenant (upsell signal vs. bug).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Junior: single events collection, query with tenantId filter, broadcast to room without tenant prefix. Breaks: (1) one bad WHERE clause = breach; (2) no per-tenant quota = noisy neighbor consumes all CPU; (3) single change stream cursor = one tenant's high-volume drowns others; (4) room name collision across tenants leaks events. Killer bug: an admin support session forgets to set tenant context in pool, runs a 'help debug' query, returns 5000 rows from across all tenants — instant breach, instant lawsuit.
